coredata.dc:title: Phase change materials in thermal management of Li-ion batteries: A state-of-the-art review

Cairns, Alasdair
coredata.dc:description: The widespread adoption of lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) in electric vehicles, portable electronics, and renewable energy systems has intensified the demand for effective thermal management strategies to ensure safety, performance, and longevity. Among emerging solutions, phase change materials (PCMs) have attracted significant interest for their passive thermal regulation capabilities, particularly their high latent heat and isothermal behavior. However, the research landscape is fragmented, with varied materials, configurations, and integration strategies explored across the literature. This review aims to provide a comprehensive synthesis of recent advances in PCM-based battery thermal management systems (BTMS), highlighting their thermal performance, structural innovations, and hybrid configurations with active cooling methods. Drawing on experimental, numerical, and theoretical studies, the review identifies key design parameters affecting efficiency, including PCM composition, nano-enhancements, encapsulation techniques, and system architecture. The review examines emerging research trends such as the use of machine learning for BTMS optimization, development of multifunctional PCMs with flame-retardant and structural properties, and cost-benefit analyses supporting commercial viability. Despite notable progress, challenges remain in material stability, scalability, and integration complexity. This review concludes by outlining future research directions, including the standardization of performance metrics, exploration of bio-based PCMs, and adaptive control strategies.

Illustration of different heat generation mechanisms in lithium-ion batteries: (a) rise in cell temperature initiating exothermic reactions, (b) electrochemical reactions under standard operating conditions, and (c) temperature-dependent chemical processes affecting battery stability. Reproduction of panels with permission: a [30,31] (Figure reproduced from Ref. [30,31]).

Configurations of battery arrangements based on size and shape, used to optimize thermal uniformity and space utilization [45] (Figure reproduced from Ref. [45]).

Example applications and working mechanism of lithium-ion batteries, illustrating energy flow in electronic and vehicular systems [65] (Figure reproduced from Ref. [65]).
Basic structure and operating principle of a lithium-ion cell, identifying key internal components and charge pathways [66] (Figure reproduced from Ref. [66]).
Charging strategy integrating pulsed preheating: (a) operational flowchart, (b) electrochemical model, and (c) battery-charger interaction [71] (Figure reproduced from Ref. [71]).
Sources of heat in lithium-ion batteries during operation, including internal resistance, environmental influence, and vehicle dynamics [76] (Figure reproduced from Ref. [76]).
Mechanism of lithium-ion migration during charge and discharge cycles in rechargeable battery cells [81] (Figure reproduced from Ref. [81]).

An overview of related studies on the effects of temperature on battery performance.

row.entry: An automated method for preheating ternary lithium batteries with high capacities.
Cell spacing, PCM thickness, heating film power, and differential heating film power all impact.

With a time consumption of 602 s, 518 s, and 394 s and a temperature differential of around 1.48 K in minimum temperatures of 253.15 K, 263.15 K, and 273.15 K, respectively, compared to conventional preheating, quick preheat requires substantially less time.
Great format NMC/C li-ion battery system in a pouch.

Mainly, the electrochemical process leads to corrosions and side reactions in the high-temperature areas of the HLH condition, which are indicated by the growth of impedances, the Cathode active material loss, and maybe the lithium inventory loss.
At low temperatures, Li-ion batteries discharge.

Compared to the conventional way of charging with a constant voltage and current, the pulse charging technique with a rate of pulse discharge of 12C and a ratio of capacity protection of 25 % may shorten the charging time at −8.5 °C by 11 %.
Temperature fluctuations and cycling affect the state of charge (SOC) deterioration.
Helping decision-makers choose the best PV generator microgrid design by providing insights into each system's economic advantages and technical efficiency.
Carrying a Li-ion battery pack through many types of driving cycles.
Integrated electro-thermal model benefits from reversible heat.

Because the model of integrated electro-thermal accounts for reversible heat, which is crucial for accurate temperature profile prediction, it is an excellent candidate for fast electro-thermal simulations of Li-ion batteries in profiles of aggressive drive, where the irreversible heat of battery alone cannot provide a reliable temperature profile prediction.

A cubic temperature polynomial provides a good approximation for the discharge capacity for a Li-ion battery. For the case of duration grows, the battery's direct current (DC) internal resistance increases and the slope gets smaller; the ideal operating temperature for a lithium-ion battery is 20–50 °C within 1 s.
A battery that uses lithium-ion and has alternate cycle rates.

Battery deterioration is accelerated in both standard and high-temperature environments using either a low or high cycle rate; the best cycle rate, resulting from the combined effects of cycle length and degradation rate, is 2C.
Three distinct varieties of lithium-ion batteries are constructed from various materials.

Compared to 75 °C, the impedances of the batteries at −40 °C are hundreds of times greater. Although all three batteries' diffusion and transfer impedances rise as temperatures drop, NMC and LTO batteries can withstand greater temperatures better than LFP.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: An overview of related studies on air cooling of li-ion batteries.

row.entry: A method of cooling down encased battery cells using forced convection with air.
There is a 1.202 °C drop in maximum battery cell temperature and a 0.883 K drop in maximum temperature deviation when using the optimum model instead of the reference model.
A new approach to air cooling that dramatically improves efficiency.
The mean Nusselt numbers (Nu) rise as the Re number does. An increase in spacing results in an increase in thermal performance (Nu).
The divergence plenum of a typical Z-type BTMS looks like a set of steps.
How the divergence plenum is affected by the number and duration of steps.

The optimal maximum temperature for the system was 324.9 K, and the cell temperature differential was 1 K, with values of 3.94 K and 5.93 K, respectively, for a 30-mm step length in a 7-step case scenario. The highest temperature was 3.61 K lower, and the maximum temperature differential was 6.01 K smaller in a 4-step model with a step length of 45 mm compared to the ordinary model of Z-type.
Electric car battery pack air-cooling system.
Impact of intake air temperature and the number of cooling fans.

A lower input air temperature resulted in a lower mean battery pack temperature, while increased cooling fans reduced the temperature differential between the cells. The most efficient and power-efficient setup was three cooling fans with an intake air temperature of 25 °C.
Applying forced air convection to a pentagonal arrangement of battery cells.
Effects of air velocity, air inlet location, and distance between intermediate cells.

With an airflow improver in place, the air velocity may be increased from 1.76 to 2.302 m/s (19 %), and the turbulence intensity can be reduced from 196 to 0.350 m2/s2 (33 %). Consequently, by using the pentagonal cell design with an airflow improver and ideal operating settings, the temperature of the cells may be decreased by 8.6 %.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: An overview of related studies on liquid cooling of li-ion batteries.

row.entry: Systems for chilling liquids using various routes.
Aspect ratio and channel count impact.
Maximum and dispersion temperatures are inversely related to the number of channels. The temperature distribution of the battery pack drops at the outset and rises as the aspect ratio goes up.
A unique immersion method for two-phase liquids.
Impact of two-phase immersion liquid cooling.
The cell temperature remained below 34 °C in all testing circumstances, and the temperature change of the battery did not surpass 5 °C, thanks to the immersion of two-phase cooling using SF33 fluid.
This module is for high-rate charging and discharging and uses a unique liquid-cooled shell construction.
The speed of coolant flow has a role.

Compared to charging, the draining process causes the battery to heat up much more. The maximum temperature of the battery module drops slightly as the coolant flow rate rises; nevertheless, the temperature differential stays constant, leading to a much higher pressure drop.
A device that uses liquid to cool lithium-ion batteries by the specifications of the cooling plate.
The impact of pumping power, heat transfer rate, and battery thermal management (BTM).

The battery module's mean temperature dropped from 53.8 to 50.7 °C when the flow rate of the cooling plate was raised from 2 to 6 L/min; however, the pumping power was raised from 0.036 to 0.808 W. As a bonus, the mean battery module temperature was lowered, and the power of pumping was reduced when the number and breadth of the cooling channels were increased.
Two different methods of cooling lithium-ion battery packs using liquids.
Impacts of input placement, mass flow rate, hole diameter, and number of channels.
For both systems, with mass flow rates greater than 5E-05 kg/s, the maximum temperature can be kept below 313 K, and the greatest temperature fluctuation can be kept below 3.15 K.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: An overview of related studies on active thermal management systems.

row.entry: Pumping a coolant via the cooling tube has an equal distribution in the form of copper.
After reducing the cell temperature by 14 °C, thermal management employing CPCM and liquid cooling became much more effective.
Consisting of heatsinks with fluid channels and materials that undergo a multi-phase transition (BTMS).
The width of the fluid channel and PCM thickness.

For the same heatsink-to-battery volume ratio, raising the thickness of PCMs minimized the temperature raise by 2.85 K and enhanced temperature uniformity compared to increasing the width of the fluid channel.
An innovative battery module with multi-height cells.
The highest temperatures for the base model are 301.5, 303.9, and 308.8 K for 1, 2, and 3C rates, whereas the revised model has temperatures of 300.6, 302.1, and 304 K, respectively.
Impact of charge and discharge rate.
With a pressure drop of 8.89 Pa and a 5.844C discharge rate, the temperature difference is just 3.86 K, and the maximum temperature is 305.09 K.
Model for bionic spider web heat dissipation using a liquid-cooled medium.

With a pressure drop of coolant of just 0.78 Pa, the highest temperature guaranteed after the melting process is 38.6 °C, and the temperature uniformity of the battery pack is maintained at roughly 2 °C. This is achieved by using a critical Reynolds number.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: An overview of related studies on passive thermal management systems.

row.entry: Module for Li-ion batteries that use passive cooling based on a metal matrix.
The effects of a passive cooling system based on a metal matrix.

The highest module temperature was decreased to 44.1 °C, and the differential in temperature was 1.6 °C when the current was at 25 A (3.2C), and the temperature was 35 °C using a passive cooling approach based on a metal matrix.
Two separate heat extraction mediums form a novel passive cooling system.

The suggested passive cooling system has the potential to achieve a maximum temperature decrease of 29.3 K, which is more than enough to keep the EV power unit at an optimal working temperature even in hot climates.
Heat pipes serve as passive cooling systems for lithium-ion batteries.
Impact of using a heat pipe.
When operating at high power output rates (e.g., 4C to 8C), a heating pipe may bring the difference in temperature between the surrounding environment and the cell down to lower than 3 °C.
Battery passive cooling using composite PCM for electric vehicles.
Effect of incorporating graphite into the composite PCM.
The composite PCM's heat absorption capacity was significantly enhanced by adding 4.6 wt% graphite, and forced air at a velocity of 20 m·s−1 outperformed PCM in terms of cooling behavior.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: An overview of related studies on hybrid techniques.

row.entry: A microchannel and phase change material (PCM) hybrid cooling system.
Impact of employing PCM/metal foam and microchannels.
Applying microchannels and PCM/metal foam leads to a differential in temperature of about 3.4 K and a highest temperature increase of 308.4 K.
A novel cooling system that combines nano and dielectric fluids is being developed.
The effects of various inflow velocities, coolants, and C-rates, as well as geometrical modifications and actual drive cycles.

The latter provides much superior results when comparing the thermal performance of water (0.2 m/s intake flow rate) with 4 % VO2 nanofluid (0.3 m/s inlet flow rate). By including separator plates in the curved cooling channels, we could lower the LIB bundle's non-homogeneity temperature by 0.16 °C and its maximum temperature by 0.61 °C.
Liquid cooling and phase change materials make up the thermal management system of hybrid batteries.
Cooling of liquids and phase-changing materials: their effects.

Even with a rapid discharge rate of 8C, the hybrid cooling system maintains a safe temperature for the module. Compared to the uniform serpentine channel, cooling performance is suitable when grooving cold plates with CTSC.
Combines heat-insulating materials, phase-change cooling, and nanofluid cooling.
Compared to plan 6, the enhanced plan achieves a 23 % reduction in maximum battery temperature and a 22 % reduction in economic index.
Lithium-ion batteries have been tested at high discharge rates using a novel TMS that integrates active and passive techniques.
For Re of 340 and a heating power of 3.7 W, the hybrid thermal management system decreased the battery's steady-state temperature by 19.5 % compared to the active technique.

Sodium thiosulfate pentahydrate maintained the cell's temperature near the melting point without using thermal conductivity enhancers due to its high thermal conductivity.

row.entry: Results showed that the battery pack's temperature can be reduced by 4.5 °C at 45 °C ambient temperature when using the hybrid BTMS.
The coolant velocity was set at 0.02 m/s for optimized thermal performance and energy consumption.

Results showed that the module temperature can be maintained below 318.15 k at a 3C discharging rate during extreme hot conditions. While in extreme cold conditions, the system can preheat the battery with a maximum rate of temperature rise of 1.333 k.
The proposed BTMS can provide an appropriate battery working condition of temperature ranging between 293.15 and 318.15 K under the ambient temperature of 268.15–313.15 K.
PCM integrated with a water plate can limit the maximum temperature and improve the uniformity of temperature distribution during five cycles

Thermal conductivity has a minor influence on the cooling process.
Increasing the mass flow rate of the water can enhance the cooling process.

Different conclusions were drawn that could be useful for future consideration in BTMS design and optimization.
Results showed that the circular configuration exhibits better thermal management performance than rectangular and hexagonal shapes.
Forced airflow with 0.2 m/s can enhance the cooling effect by 642 % compared to natural cooling.
liquid cold plate with a Z-type cooling channel

The findings reveal that the optimal design of the hybrid cold plate, weighing only half as much as the baseline, can achieve a greater than 50 % decrease in overall pumping consumption while maintaining equivalent cooling performance.
The hybrid BTMS has the potential to yield an annual energy savings of 252.5 kWh per vehicle.
Hexagonal cooling plate with bionic liquid mini channel
The hexagonal cooling plate is better than the rectangular one, which can further reduce the maximum temperature by 0.36 k.
A neural network is also proposed by the authors in this study to estimate the inlet flow rate of the battery module based on different conditions.
battery pack contains 1 × 6 prismatic LiFeO4 battery cells.
Results showed that 30 % of the temperature difference can be reduced after applying the optimization technique.
The proposed optimization method can be extended to include different factors such as types and numbers of battery cells, types of PCMs, operation parameters, and structural parameters.
Results showed that different melting points of PCM do not highly affect the temperature difference between batteries.
The authors stated that the latent heat of PCM should be utilized to ensure the BTMS operates appropriately.
Micro-encapsulated phase change material with polyethylene and expanded graphite.
The proposed BTMS can save up to 79.9 % of the total energy.
The optimal parameters of the proposed BTMS are using water with ethanol at an inlet velocity of 0.193 m/s that can make the maximum temperature of the battery module at 33.12 °C.

row.entry: Selection and compatibility with battery systems
Medium to high depending on material stability
Low to high (nanomaterials and hybrids increase cost)
Cost of advanced materials and processing
Low to high depending on base material
Safety concerns, especially with paraffin-based PCMs
Consumer electronics, EVs, grid storage, aerospace
Performance trade-offs depending on environment and load

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A summary of how PCMs affect the thermal performance of Li-ion batteries.

row.entry: An experimental investigation of employing porous metal foam infused with PCMs to TM of Li-ion batteries.
Effect of pure PCM, copper foam, porosity, and pore density on the temperature surface of the battery

Pure PCM led to a notable reduction in the battery's surface temperature.
Incorporating copper foam and paraffin led to a further reduction in battery temperature.
The rise in porosity and pore density led to elevated temperatures on the battery surface.
TM of Li-ion batteries by using Graphene-enhanced hybrid PCMs
Effects of using graphene-enhanced hybrid PCMs on Li-ion battery temperature.

Including graphene enhances the thermal conductivity of PCMs.
When graphene is added, the temperature rise in Li-ion batteries is significantly decreased.
The impact of form-stable composite PCMs in a flexible form on Li-ion battery TM.
Li-ion batteries temperature, Battery operating duration

When flexible PCM is used, the battery temperature drops quickly.
The battery pack's operating life is significantly increased.
Numerical investigation of the effect of PCM on Li-ion battery TM.
The temperature gradient throughout the battery.

Passive, inexpensive battery TM systems using PCMs can efficiently reduce the highest temperatures and temperature differentials, improving heat storage, equalization, and temperature retention functions.
Utilizing inorganic composite PCMs to manage the thermal conditions of Li-ion batteries

Inorganic PCMs prove to be effective for thermal control in Li-ion batteries.
The batteries are kept at consistent temperatures within a safe range.
Optimizing thermal performance through composite graphite PCM for Li-ion battery
The graphite weight effect on Li-ion batteries temperature.
CPCM with 16–20 wt% expanded graphite shows Li-ion batteries' most promising TM performance, enhancing thermal conductivity and reducing temperature rise.
Modeling and optimizing PCM TM for a Li-ion battery pack through analytical methods.
The influence of PCM thermal conductivity.
While phase change cooling successfully lowers the cell's temperature on the outside, the cell core stays heated, necessitating increased thermal conductivity for best results.
Investigation into TM of a cylindrical Li-ion battery through the use of PCM composites in an experimental setting
Impact of macroscopic and nanoscale materials incorporated into PCMs on the TM of Li-ion batteries.

Metal matrix-PCM composites with Ag nanoparticles show the best thermal performance for managing temperature distribution in lithium-ion batteries, decreasing maximum temperature differences by up to 70 %.
Experimental investigation into the utilization of hybrid carbon-based PCMs for enhancing the TM performance of a Li-ion battery module.
The influence of hybrid carbon-based PCMs

The maximum battery module temperatures are decreased by 7.17 °C, 10.52 °C, and 16.33 °C thanks to the hybrid carbon-based PCMs with 1 % few-layer graphene and 2 % graphite nanoplatelet, which enhance thermal conductivity and cooling impact in Li-ion battery modules.
TM systems for electric vehicle batteries utilizing PCMs.
PCMs offer a cheaper and easier alternative for TM of electric vehicle batteries, potentially enabling near-autonomous operations.

head.ce:title: Phase change materials in thermal management of Li-ion batteries: A state-of-the-art review

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The widespread adoption of lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) in electric vehicles, portable electronics, and renewable energy systems has intensified the demand for effective thermal management strategies to ensure safety, performance, and longevity. Among emerging solutions, phase change materials (PCMs) have attracted significant interest for their passive thermal regulation capabilities, particularly their high latent heat and isothermal behavior. However, the research landscape is fragmented, with varied materials, configurations, and integration strategies explored across the literature. This review aims to provide a comprehensive synthesis of recent advances in PCM-based battery thermal management systems (BTMS), highlighting their thermal performance, structural innovations, and hybrid configurations with active cooling methods. Drawing on experimental, numerical, and theoretical studies, the review identifies key design parameters affecting efficiency, including PCM composition, nano-enhancements, encapsulation techniques, and system architecture. The review examines emerging research trends such as the use of machine learning for BTMS optimization, development of multifunctional PCMs with flame-retardant and structural properties, and cost-benefit analyses supporting commercial viability. Despite notable progress, challenges remain in material stability, scalability, and integration complexity. This review concludes by outlining future research directions, including the standardization of performance metrics, exploration of bio-based PCMs, and adaptive control strategies.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The greenhouse effect and air pollution have gained sustained attention in recent years with the rapid advancement of the economy [1,2]. As a result of this environmental pollution problem, the global climate has become abnormally warmer [3,4]. Oil is a relatively inefficient means of utilizing resources in vehicles. Therefore, methods for energy conservation can reduce excessive energy use without demanding additional utility or service capacity to achieve such a result [5,6]. In terms of the installed storage capacity, it is now at 230 GW, with pumped hydro accounting for 96 % of this capacity, which has limited availability [5]. In terms of installed storage capacity, there is only 170 GW, with pumped hydro accounting for 96 % of this capacity, which is limited in availability. A wide range of battery technologies is required to meet the widely varying needs for large-scale electrical storage [6,7].

Due to their high energy density, power density, and longer lifespan, lithium-ion batteries (LIB) are increasingly popular in consumer electronics and electric vehicles (EVs) because of their environmental friendliness [8]. The remarkable advantages of LIBs as supporting devices in the grid have attracted considerable attention among various battery technologies. These advantages include high energy density (up to 200 Wh/kg), high efficiency (more than 95 %), and long cycle life. Batteries, supercapacitors, and fuel cells have all been used as energy storage devices (ESDs) in electric vehicles (EVs). Batteries store electricity electrochemically, while supercapacitors store electrical charge primarily through hydrogen (H2). A voltage of 1.5 to 5.5 V can be achieved by connecting ESD cells in parallel and series for meeting the power requirement of EVs [10–12]. As part of our modern industrial revolution, electric vehicles (EVs) are at the forefront of the drive towards zero CO2 emissions. Hopefully, this effort will minimize the global warming phenomenon and safeguard our planet from its consequences in the next decade. Various factors must be considered, including the electricity mix used [9–12].

LIB use in EVs has numerous environmental and technological advantages, including zero tailpipe emissions during operation, noise and vibration reduction, and low maintenance [16–19]. RB family batteries, more particularly metal-ion batteries like popular lithium batteries and zinc-ion, magnesium-ion, aluminum-ion, and sodium-ion batteries, are a few of the promising upcoming technologies [20,21]. Lithium-ion batteries are limited in performance and cost when storing large amounts of electrical energy. Consequently, stationary applications necessitate large-scale battery storage. This review compares well-developed battery chemistry and design, including recent advances. Furthermore, it discusses potential improvements and the challenges that still need to be addressed [13,14]. Remember that batteries are prone to serious issues, such as short circuits and excessive ambient temperatures, which can cause overheating and failure [15]. To maintain the battery temperature within a specified range, EVs and HEVs must develop an efficient cooling and heating system. Substantial research efforts have been spent working on efficient BTMS, and there are many available methodologies. The methodologies involve different kinds of systems, such as active or passive systems, series or parallel setups, internal or external means for heating or cooling, liquid or air-based systems, the use of heat pipes, and PCMs [25–34].

The rapid expansion of electric vehicles (EVs), portable electronics, and renewable energy storage systems has amplified the global demand for lithium-ion (Li-ion) batteries. While these batteries offer high energy density, long cycle life, and efficient charge-discharge characteristics, their thermal sensitivity poses a critical challenge to safe and reliable operation [16]. Excessive heat generation during high-rate charging or discharging can result in accelerated degradation, performance loss, and, in severe cases, thermal runaway and catastrophic failure. Therefore, advanced battery thermal management systems (BTMS) are indispensable to maintain cell temperatures within optimal operating limits, ensure uniform temperature distribution, and extend battery lifespan [17].

Among various thermal management strategies, phase change materials (PCMs) have emerged as promising passive solutions due to their ability to absorb and store large amounts of latent heat during phase transition [18]. Their inherent capacity to regulate temperature without requiring active power input makes them highly suitable for compact and energy-efficient BTMS configurations. This review paper focuses on the evolving role of PCMs in Li-ion battery thermal management, highlighting recent advancements in material enhancement (e.g., nano-additives, composite structures), system integration, and hybridization with active cooling methods. By evaluating current trends, performance metrics, and implementation challenges, the study aims to provide a comprehensive foundation for the design and application of PCM-based BTMS in next-generation energy storage systems [19].

Among the various passive and active thermal management strategies, phase change materials (PCMs) have emerged as a promising solution for BTMS due to their ability to absorb and release large amounts of latent heat during phase transitions [20]. This property allows PCMs to regulate temperature spikes and maintain thermal uniformity within the battery pack, especially under high charge-discharge rates or extreme ambient conditions. When integrated into BTMS architectures (either as standalone modules or in combination with active cooling mechanisms) PCMs can effectively mitigate thermal runaway, enhance system safety, and extend battery lifespan without the need for continuous external energy input. The compatibility of PCMs with various battery formats and system configurations makes them a flexible and scalable component in the design of next-generation thermal management systems [21].

Dispersion of nanoparticles in phase change materials (PCMs) has been discovered to increase the active efficiency of battery systems. There is a definite section to discuss the limitations regarding thermal conductivity of PCMs. The paper presents a detailed study and synthesis of hybridized composite PCMs for optimizing the performance of batteries. Future research in electric vehicles (EVs) could focus on this area [22]. Typically, phase change materials (PCMs) with a melting temperature of around 50 °C are used in battery research. The main advantages of PCMs are temperature regulation and high latent heat. When the cell temperature is higher than its melting point, the PCM is able to absorb heat isothermally. Conversely, the PCM can discharge heat isothermally if the battery temperature is low [36,37]. Batteries, with the help of BTMSs, can deliver their best performance at the best temperature, ensuring enhanced safety and stability. In this article, a thorough discussion is made on BTMSs, battery materials, and the temperature aspect in the scenario of fast charging [38]. In this paper, the basic principles of BTMS are explained, including aspects such as battery working, temperature impact, and modeling approaches affecting battery performance. Furthermore, common battery cooling techniques are reviewed and classified in terms of the types of working fluids employed and their working principles [39].

The bibliometric analysis was conducted using the Scopus database, covering publications from 2013 to 2024. The search keywords included “phase change material,” “battery thermal management,” “lithium-ion battery,” and “PCM-based cooling.” The retrieved data were analyzed using VOSviewer (version 1.6.20) to construct a keyword co-occurrence network, with a minimum threshold of five occurrences per keyword. This approach enabled the identification of the dominant research themes and their interconnections within the PCM-based BTMS literature. Fig. 1 includes four distinct color-coded clusters: (1) red – PCM material enhancement and nanocomposites, (2) blue – hybrid and multi-mode cooling systems, (3) green – numerical modeling and optimization studies, and (4) yellow – emerging data-driven and multifunctional PCM innovations. These clusters directly informed the structural organization of this review: Section 7 explores material and system integration (red cluster), Section 4.5 addresses hybrid techniques (blue cluster), Section 6 focuses on modeling and simulation (green cluster), and Section 9 discusses machine learning and multifunctional PCMs (yellow cluster). Establishing this correspondence strengthens the analytical continuity between the bibliometric mapping and the thematic development of the paper, ensuring that the review structure reflects the actual research landscape in this field.

While numerous review articles have explored PCM applications in battery thermal management, many of these are either narrowly focused on conventional materials or lack depth in emerging strategies such as hybrid systems and nano-enhanced composites. Moreover, few reviews systematically address intelligent optimization methods (such as machine learning) or assess the commercial feasibility of these technologies. This review aims to fill these critical gaps by integrating recent advancements in nano-enhanced and multifunctional PCMs, highlighting the role of AI-driven design, and evaluating the cost-benefit considerations essential for real-world implementation. Through this comprehensive approach, the review contributes a timely and holistic perspective on the development of next-generation PCM-based BTMS.

The thermal behavior of lithium-ion batteries significantly impacts their operational effectiveness, safety, and lifespan. With the increasing demand for high-energy density and effective power solutions, the relevance of lithium-ion batteries has increased significantly. Lithium-ion batteries are employed in various applications ranging from electric vehicles to grid energy storage systems. The thermal behavior of lithium-ion batteries directly impacts their performance, efficiency, and safety [23].

Li-ion batteries generate heat due to the electrochemical reactions in the battery when they undergo charge and discharge cycles. Effective dissipation of heat prevents thermal runaway, premature aging, and violent failures such as fire or explosion. Moreover, temperature variations influence the capacity, power delivery, and cycle life of the batteries [41]. A lithium-ion battery's thermal runaway is mainly caused by heat generated after it overheats. Thermal runaway is illustrated in Fig. 2 as a chain reaction that releases energy [24].

A working temperature below zero degrees Celsius is considered State I. In this situation, the electrochemical reactions could reduce dramatically, and the capacity, rate of performance, etc., could also be further restricted. Additionally, the lithiation reaction of graphite on the anode can easily cause Li plating and dendrites to be triggered during the charging process, which can penetrate the separator and trigger ISC within the lithium-ion battery due to the lithiation reaction of graphite [25]. In State II, the lithium-ion battery operates normally, with a heating rate that is typically less than one degree Celsius per minute. The critical temperature in this state is typically around 90 degrees Celsius. The heat generation is the result of a combination of electrochemical, ohmic, polarized, and mixed reactions. It is likely that lithium-ion batteries can suffer from a decrease in lifespan at temperatures above 40 °C due to increased side reactions. Also, when the temperature is as high as 90 °C or higher, the SEI film decomposes to induce thermal runaway, which is characterized by the onset of decomposition [44].

During State III, there is a high temperature rise of more than 1 °C per minute but no higher than 10 °C per minute. Several side reactions occur during this stage, mainly including the decomposition of the SEI film, reactions between Li and organic solvents, melting of the separators, and the decomposition of the cathode materials [26].

In State IV, the electrodes are heated at a rate greater than 10 degrees Celsius per minute, causing chemical crosstalk between them. Additionally, the electrolyte burns and explodes in this state [27].

Heat generation in States I and II is relatively small compared to the thermal runaway process. As shown in Fig. 2, b, heat generation in a normal state is primarily the result of Li + diffusion in solids and liquids, electrochemical reactions at solid-liquid interfaces, and side reactions. As a result of this heat generation, the temperature inside the battery rises, and the temperature difference between the inside and outside may hurt its lifespan and safety. During low-temperature charging, the lithium plating on the anode is subject to a significant increase in self-heating due to the aging process at high currents [28].

The primary heat generation in States III and IV results from the A thermal runaway within lithium-ion batteries due to overheating, which happens after the onset of certain issues. Fig. 2 illustrates the chain reactions in a thermal runaway process and the release of energy during the process [29].

This study sets out on an in-depth experimental and theoretical exploration to examine the thermal behavior of lithium-ion batteries. By assessing the heat generation, dissipation, and temperature distribution properties of these batteries, a closer approximation of the fundamental mechanisms and constraints relating to thermal performance can be determined. Such insights will contribute to advancing our knowledge of Li-ion batteries and improving their thermal management strategies [32].

As shown in Fig. 3 , lithium-ion batteries are prone to a range of abuse conditions—thermal, electrical, and mechanical—that can trigger a chain of failure events that ultimately culminate in thermal runaway. This figure is especially pertinent to the BTMS design process insofar as it demonstrates the key role of thermal management in severing this chain of events, particularly in response to fast-charging or external heating. Knowledge of these pathways is essential for formulating PCM-based solutions that will not only alleviate heat accumulation but also its systemic effects throughout the battery pack. This study was based on the reaction kinetics and thermal properties of lithium-ion batteries sourced from the literature [33].

Similarly, the electrode-electrolyte interface is also affected by low temperatures in LIBs. The solid electrolyte interface's (SEI) overpotential increases with decreasing temperature [35]. There is a difference between Li + and SEI in terms of their movement through the SEI film in a semiconductor device. Li + has to overcome a higher energy barrier, which limits its transport and increases the charge transfer resistance (Rct) when it does so. Additionally, when the electrolyte is exposed to low temperatures, its viscosity can grow or even cause solidification, further adversely affecting its compatibility with the separator and electrode, as well as the formation of the cathode-electrolyte interphase and the electrolyte phase interphase [36]. For example, a commercial electrolyte like an EC-based electrolyte cannot be used below 0 °C. On the other hand, a low-temperature environment may lead to deterioration of the anode due to lithium plating and an increased formation of Li dendrites and “dead” Li during cycling. Insufficient attention to this aspect can result in volume expansion and destruction of the internal structure of LIBs, as well as short circuits and thermal runaway. Therefore, the safety of LIBs would be seriously compromised (See Fig. 4 ).

Recent findings regarding electrolytes, cathodes, and anodes used in LIBs are reviewed to enhance their performance at low temperatures. Our overview is attempting to fundamentally comprehend the key determinants of the low-temperature performance of lithium-ion batteries from a materials perspective [38]. As illustrated in Fig. 5 , low-temperature conditions trigger a number of coupled electrochemical issues in lithium-ion batteries. These are limited Li+ diffusion through the solid electrolyte interphase (SEI), high charge transfer resistance (Rct), and physical processes like electrolyte viscosity increase and possible crystallization. In addition, mismatched interfacial compatibility between the electrolyte and electrode materials at low temperatures causes capacity loss and heightened risk of lithium plating. Such degradation mechanisms highlight the relevance of temperature regulation strategies—like PCM-based BTMS—that guarantee safe and efficient battery operation over a broad temperature spectrum.

The construction of a lithium-ion battery entails several integral parts that coordinate with each other in a manner that stores and releases electrical energy functionally. They consist of positive electrodes (cathodes), negative electrodes (anodes), separators, electrolytes, and current collectors [40]. The components each have a determining role in the overall functionality and efficiency of the battery. Lithium-ion batteries consist of numerous elements, and each element holds significance to the proper functioning of the battery with high efficiency [41].

To enhance the performance and safety of lithium-ion batteries, it is important to understand the structural organization and characteristics of these components. This design and material used in each component significantly impact energy storage capacity, cycling stability, rate performance, and thermal characteristics [42].

This review provides an overview of the components and structure of Li-ion batteries. To better understand Li-ion batteries, examining their composition, functions, and interactions is essential. Such knowledge can improve Li-ion battery technologies, enhance performance, and ensure safety [43]. A disaster may occur in the event of a battery explosion caused by thermal runaway [44]. For the battery to function correctly, there should be a temperature difference of less than 5 degrees Celsius between the cell and the module, which is crucial for maintaining a safe working environment, as shown in Fig. 6 and Fig. 7 . Fig. 7 shows the design progress of a 180 Ah lithium-ion battery cell. In its initial design (Fig. 7a), the cell has a traditional stacked or wound internal structure, which tends to experience non-uniform heat dissipation and the creation of thermal hotspots under high-rate operation. To rectify this, a laminated core structure was developed (Fig. 7b), enhancing lateral thermal conductivity and enabling more uniform heat spreading between electrode layers. The design was further optimized in Fig. 7c by streamlining the internal structure to minimize thermal resistance and better accommodate external cooling approaches. These structural improvements strongly enable the integration of passive BTMS methods, such as PCMs, by allowing more homogeneous temperature fields and more favorable overall thermal response under charge/discharge cycling.

The absence of specific components is one of the factors contributing to the success of lithium-ion batteries, as is the case with other battery technologies. Lithium ions are transferred between the positive and negative electrodes in a general charge storage mechanism. Fig. 8 illustrates the state-of-the-art components of a lithium battery, where these electrodes act as host matrices for lithium ions. Consequently, the performance metrics and overall properties of lithium-ion batteries are primarily dictated by the selection of electrochemically active and inactive materials [47]. The choice and formulation of electrochemically active materials have a governing influence on the battery's performance and operation, while electrochemically inactive components, such as the current collector, are to assume a supporting role.

A polymer binder mainly strengthens electrodes' mechanical stability, particle adhesion, and flexibility by binding the active material and conductive additives together. Electrode conductivity is improved by the conductive additive, whereas cell capacity and potential are determined by the active material. Fig. 9 illustrates an electrode and its principal constituents schematically [49].

Fig. 10 demonstrates that lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) have several components and functions, like computers. By applying an external electrical potential during the charging process, lithium ions are deintercalated from the cathode's intercalated state and pass through the electrolyte to be intercalated in the anode. This displacement of lithium ions causes the generation of an electron current, which flows from the cathode to the anode. The cathode and anode, composed partly of aluminum and partly copper, respectively, conduct current. During discharge, the displacement of electrons and ions is reversed. The electrochemical performance of LIBs is strongly influenced by the materials and modification methods incorporated in the electrode structures [51,52].

Lithium batteries are able to transform chemical energy into electrical energy via electrochemical oxidation and reduction (redox) reactions. The same characteristic enables lithium-ion batteries to transform electrical energy into chemical energy, making them secondary batteries. The reaction's reversibility makes lithium-ion batteries reversible batteries. In contrast, primary batteries are irreversibly converted into electrical energy, while secondary batteries store chemical energy [54].

Green batteries have always been preferred, and LIBs are among the most popular. Due to continuous improvements in production technology and ongoing cost reductions, lithium-ion batteries are now widely used in various fields Fig. 11 . In addition to applications in solar power stations, wind power, firepower, and water power, lithium batteries are extensively utilized as portable power storage systems, power tools, military equipment, aerospace applications, and more [55–57]. Currently, there is a gradual expansion of the use of LIBs in other areas, such as electric bicycles and electric cars [58,59].

When charging, lithium ions are drawn out of the positive electrode particles and passed through the electrolyte and separator to the negative side [60]. To keep the electric field balance on the surface of the positively charged electrode particles, the same number of electrons must be emitted at this surface simultaneously [61]. These electrons are then collected by a positive current collector to create the charge current, which flows through a circuit from the positive electrode to the negative electrode.
Finally, the procedure ends in a reaction between electrons and lithium ions, allowing their intercalation into the materials of the positive electrode [62,63].

In the discharge process, the electrochemical reaction is the reverse of that during the charging process. An equal number of lithium ions and electrons travel to the positive electrode by traversing the internal and external circuits [64].

In addition to liquid electrolytes, Li-ion batteries have also utilized polymer, gel, and ceramic electrolytes. Fig. 12 illustrates the basic operating principle of Li-ion battery cells. Despite exploring different electrode materials, electrolytes, and separators over the past two decades, Li-ion cells remain fundamentally unchanged and operate on the same principles as they did two decades ago [66].

Charging process involves the transfer of electric power into a recoverable storage device in a device, typically a battery. Whilst the fast Charging is a specific technology employed in battery charging that reduces the time to charge batteries over conventional methods. Li-ion batteries move lithium ions from the positive to the negative electrodes while charging. This is achieved through a reverse electrochemical process where the lithium ions are compelled to shift from the positive terminal to the negative terminal when an external power source subjects the battery to a voltage that is greater than the battery's nominal voltage. At the same time, the battery takes in electrical energy and thus increases the charge stored in the battery [67,68]. To value the effect of fast charging on lithium-ion battery performance and safety, it is important to consider the mechanisms involved, charging rates, heat generation, and thermal management strategies [69,70].

Fig. 13 shows the schematic framework of the model as well as the charging strategy we propose. Based on the volume-average hypothesis, we consider that the anode and cathode consist of porous, spherical particles as the active material. The porous particles contain interstitial voids filled with electrolytes. Within the P2D model framework, the interfaces between the solid and liquid phases are ignored, and therefore there is no capacity to differentiate between these two phases. This simplification lowers the complexity of the simulation considerably and, as a result, the computational demands.

The heating of cells has been cited multiple times by researchers as a major cause of cell degradation. A cell's temperature increases when it exceeds a fixed operating range. If cell temperatures continue to rise without proper monitoring and control, it can lead to thermal runaway [72].

Thermal conductivity, environmental conditions, and self-heating are the major factors contributing to battery heating [73]. The design components of the battery pack, such as bus bars and heat sinks, can cause heating due to their thermal conductivity [74]. In addition to the internal resistance in cells, self-heating occurs due to the combined effects of vehicle design, driving patterns, and battery management systems (BMS). The cell resistance, represented by I2R losses, leads to increased heating. When driving patterns involve steep accelerations and decelerations, the inrush of current in the pack further contributes to heating [75]. Additionally, exposure to hotter climatic conditions can cause the battery pack to heat up through conduction. Fig. 14 illustrates that fast-charging leads to higher heating in the battery pack due to the high inrush of current.

The discharge mechanism of lithium-ion batteries is key to the operation of portable electronic devices and electric vehicles. Lithium-ion batteries' high energy density, extended cycle life, and comparatively low self-discharge rate have placed them ahead of other batteries as the preferred choice. To enhance battery performance, prolong its lifespan, and guarantee dependable performance, a clear comprehension of the discharge mechanism is of great importance [77].

This research aims to carry out a comprehensive examination of the discharging process of lithium-ion batteries with the aim of establishing its cause and effect. This work examines the discharging behavior of Li-ion batteries, voltage profiles, and how variables such as temperature and DOD affect them. Such knowledge will enhance Li-ion battery technology, improve discharge strategies and battery management systems, and enhance performance and reliability [78,79].

Lithium-ion batteries transfer lithium ions from the cathode to the anode through an electrolyte separator during the charging process and move back from the anode to the cathode during discharging, as depicted in Fig. 15 . Li-ion batteries employ compound lithium electrodes instead of metallic lithium, which are used in non-rechargeable batteries. It is worth noting that most consumer electronics products now incorporate Li-ion batteries [80].

Fig. 16 illustrates the discharge capacity and cycle number of lithium-ion batteries. The discharge capacity of lithium-ion batteries decreases rapidly at 1C charge-discharge rates. Even at lower charge-discharge rates, the battery capacity drops from 1005 to 700 mAh after 286 cycles. The results of the present study suggest that certain components of the battery change from cycling, particularly the interfaces that connect the solid electrodes to the nonaqueous electrolyte. To better understand these changes, three lithium-ion batteries were measured at different cycles to compare the impedance spectra of the batteries [82].

There is no doubt that lithium is one of the most reactive metals due to its highly active electronic structure, which is a result of having the highest charge-to-mass ratio among all metal elements. As a result of its high theoretical energy density, lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) are at the forefront. However, it is worth noting that LIBs also present a high safety risk [84,85].

The maximum heat energy is generated at the centre of the vertical axis of cylinder-type Li-ion batteries, with the battery's surface also being heated. Fig. 17 presents an example of the temperature distribution on a cylindrical cell [86]. In battery packs with high interconnection, the thermal coupling among cells is strong because of proximity, and the capacity for heat generation is high [87]. Consequently, management of temperature increase inside the cells as well as distribution of heat within the overall module and pack evenly to enhance the life of the battery is pivotal. Failure to address temperature problems may cause thermal runaway with attendant explosions or fires and serious safety risks if left unchecked [88].

Lithium-ion batteries primarily operate by shuttling lithium ions between the positive and negative electrodes of the battery [90]. Lithium ions detach from the negative electrode during discharging and pass through the electrolyte to reach the positive electrode. Charging is the reverse discharge process when the device is used [91]. The charging and discharging of lithium-ion batteries involve an electrochemical reaction that requires time to complete and is accompanied by the conversion of energy and the generation of heat as part of the process [92].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Importance of thermal management in Li-ion batteries
ce:section.ce:para: Thermal management is the system and procedures to keep a system's temperature where it needs to be. To avoid electrical equipment overheating, thermal management usually removes surplus heat [93]. Too much thermal energy may destroy delicate internal components of most electronic systems, which is why heat generation is inevitable. Similarly, electronics are susceptible to harm when exposed to high temperatures outside [94]. Engineers in several sectors, such as data centers, batteries, aircraft, and automobiles, use thermal management systems. Temperature management is one of any design's most important and intricate parts. When designing a system, engineers consider its specific characteristics to provide the best possible performance, lifespan, and durability. Cooling methods, including fans and heat sinks, are among the most widely used thermal management techniques [95]. To quickly lower the temperature, some liquid-cooled systems use cryogenic fluids. Thermal engineers may use cooling methods and insulating materials to target vulnerable locations and eliminate or significantly decrease heat transfer rates to such areas [96].

The lithium-ion battery (LIB) is now the go-to option for electric vehicle (EV) energy storage due to its many benefits, including being lightweight, having a high energy density, having a long cycle life, having a low self-discharge rate, and not having any memory effect [97]. Nevertheless, LIB temperatures will inevitably increase under high-power or acceleration, reducing cell performance, life, and safety and perhaps leading to thermal runaway issues. Therefore, the battery thermal management system is essential for keeping the high-power lithium-ion batteries within their optimal working temperature range [98]. The ideal working temperature range for LIBs, which keeps the temperature differential of batteries below 5 °C, is 20 °C to 40 °C, and the permitted operating temperature range is −20 °C to 60 °C [99]. Many cooling techniques may be used to meet thermal management needs, guarantee operational dependability, and reduce safety threats. These strategies include heat pipes, liquid cooling, air cooling, phase change materials (PCMs), and combinations of these systems [100].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of temperature on battery performance
ce:section.ce:para: A technique of unique coupling preheating for phase change material (PCM) and heating film is applied to ensure preheating in the large-capacity ternary lithium battery proposed by Jiaqiang et al. (2023) [101]. To ensure that the preheating system can fulfill the battery pack's preheating needs, numerical studies are conducted to examine the impact of four parameters: PCM thickness, cell spacing, heating film power difference, and heating film power. With a time, consumption of 602 s, 518 s, and 394 s and temperature differential of around 1.48 K for minimum temperatures of 253.15 K, 263.15 K, and 273.15 K, respectively, fast preheating significantly reduces preheating time compared to traditional preheating.

Applying a newly-developed instrument that may actively manage suitable longitudinal gradients and concurrently monitor the heat generation rate (HGR), Du et al. (2023) [102] experimentally studied the effects of temperature gradients on the life performance of a large-format cell. Here, the performance under non-uniform and uniform temperature settings is compared and quantified. In the early stages of life (BOL), the cell capacity is primarily constrained by the minimum-temperature areas of the LMH and HLH conditions, whereas the HLH condition inhibits the HGR at high C-rates. Cells undergo a more pronounced capacity fading and an increase in HGR under HLH conditions compared to MMM conditions as they age. According to the results of the impedance analyses, half-coin cell, dynamic resistance, and differential capacity, the main mechanisms that cause the HLH condition's high-temperature side reactions and corrosions are the Cathode active material loss, which may also lead to the increase of impedances and the loss of lithium inventory.

Pulse charging enhances charging time and battery performance at low ambient temperatures, according to Liu and Wang (2023) [103], who used both experimental testing and numerical modeling to study the topic. To avoid over-discharging, pulse charging is characterized by a capacity protection ratio, the ratio of the remaining charging capacity to the overall charging capacity. When considering the whole charging duration, the ratio of capacity protection becomes more apparent. The pulse charging method's operating principle is investigated using thermal-linked and electrochemical models. The findings of the experiment demonstrate that, in comparison to the conventional way of charging with a constant voltage and current, the pulse charging technique with a rate of pulse discharge of 12C and a ratio of capacity protection of 25 % may shorten the charging time by 11 % at −8.5 °C. The model findings demonstrate that pulse charging improves battery performance by making the lithium-ion distribution more uniform.

Two popular technologies for battery, lead-acid (LA) and lithium-ion (LI) batteries, were the subject of an effective multi-year technical-economic comparison study by Fouzia et al. [104]. The effects of temperature cycling and fluctuations on the charge state deterioration and performance of other system components in lithium-ion and lead-acid batteries over long periods were studied. In the one-year model, the best system configuration included li-ion storage of 25.1 kW of PV and 65 kWh. The ability to store energy, efficiency, trends in deterioration, maintenance needs, and replacement cycles are all part of the study. The economic analysis includes all three factors (start-up costs, ongoing expenses, and sustainability). Researchers hope that by comparing the systems' technical efficiency and economic advantages, decision-makers would be better equipped to choose the best PV generator microgrid design.

Shahjalal et al. [105] introduced a general model for electro-thermal batteries to forecast cell temperature quickly and accurately. The suggested model incorporates heating entropy, which rectifies the model of thermal inaccuracy caused by irreversible heat, in contrast to state-of-the-art models of electro-thermal Li-ion batteries. In addition, building a thermal model is as easy as doing a few tests. Using pouch cells of 4.2 V 54 Ah Li-NMC, the experimental validation of the suggested model was carried out. Under these circumstances, the experimental findings and the results of the simulations are highly concordant, with RMSE errors of 0.15, 0.07, 0.29, and 0.26 correspondingly. For fast electro-thermal simulations of Li-ion batteries under profiles of aggressive drive, where the battery's irreversible heat is not enough to predict an accurate temperature profile, this indicates that including reversible heat in the model of integrated electro-thermal is crucial for an accurate profile of temperature prediction. Conducting tests with varying discharge and charge profiles is another way to test the electro-thermal model. The C/10 and charge profile have been deemed as having a modest charge/discharge profile in this instance. The findings of the modeling and the experiments are shown in Fig. 18 at air temperatures of 23 °C.

In an effort to replicate the thermal environment that a power battery is subjected to during use, Lv et al. [106] conducted a comparative analysis of the charging and discharging of three kinds of lithium-ion batteries: manganite, cobalt oxide, and iron phosphate. The results indicate that the discharge capacity of lithium-ion batteries can be accurately estimated using a cubic polynomial function of temperature. With the passage of time, the direct current (DC) internal resistance of the battery increases, and the slope becomes less; the optimum working temperature of a lithium-ion battery is 20–50 °C in 1 s. A linear relationship between time and the DC inner battery resistance is observed from 1 s to 10 s. At both zero state of charge (SOC) and 100 % SOC, the internal resistance of the battery is greatest both during charging and discharging. Fig. 19 presents some temperature profiles illustrating the discharge capacity of a lithium manganite battery.

The effect of hot surroundings on the rate of ideal cycle for lithium batteries was studied by Ouyang et al. [107]. There is a total rate of four cycles (0.5C, 1C, 2C, and 3C) and two surrounding temperatures—26 and 70 °C. Fig. 20 shows that the deterioration rate and cycle length are connected effects and that an ideal cycle rate is 2C under high and average temperatures. It has also been demonstrated that a low cycle rate will worsen battery degradation. Surprisingly, the high-temperature surroundings may aid in cycling high-rate water compared to normal-temperature water. Delta V, internal resistance, surface temperature, and discharge/charge voltage development further show deterioration behaviours.

In order to determine the impedance at various temperatures, Ma et al. [108] investigated three distinct types of lithium-ion batteries constructed from various materials. Effect of temperature on the impedance of various batteries were evaluated by establishing an equivalent circuit model (ECM). Temperature is a crucial metric to consider when forecasting the state of batteries. According to the findings, the impedances of the batteries are hundreds of times greater at −40 °C compared to 75 °C. Although all three batteries' diffusion and transfer impedances rise as temperatures drop, NMC and LTO batteries can withstand greater temperatures better than LFP. It can be concluded from Fig. 21 that EIS significantly impacts the state estimate and effectiveness assessment of batteries in conjunction with temperature. Table 1 shows an overview of related studies on the effects of temperature on battery performance.

Mechanical, electrical, and thermal abuse are the three main causes of lithium-ion battery failure. Some examples of applicable standards that have collected these three forms of abuse include GB/T 31485, IEC62133, SAE-J2464, UN R100, and UN 38.3. However, as producers strive to fully leverage the electrochemical potential of the battery's output, new misuse circumstances are becoming [109]. As seen in Fig. 22 , fresh findings point to electrochemical abuse as the fundamental mechanism behind the emergence of battery failure. A relatively new kind of abuse, electrochemical abuse occurs when an electrochemical substance is used in a way that causes it to function above its capacity [110]. Mechanical, electrical, and thermal abuse circumstances may be further characterized based on a better knowledge of electrochemical abuse conditions. For example, (1) when the separator's thickness is reduced to achieve a higher energy density, the electrodes' edges might touch it, leading to internal short-circuiting; (2) accidents involving lithium plating from very rapid charging have occurred; and (3) thermal runaway can occur from oxygen released from the cathode during slight overcharging while in use. When developing new rules and regulations, it is helpful to keep the severity of electrochemical abuse in mind [111,112].

Li-Ion batteries have an organic liquid electrolyte that is inherently combustible. Cascading thermal runaway events are among the most disastrous LIB system failures and are thought to be the primary source of battery safety problems [113,114]. When an exothermic process becomes uncontrolled, it is often called a thermal runaway. A positive feedback loop is created when the battery's temperature reaches around 80 °C since this causes the exothermic chemical reaction rate inside the battery to increase, which in turn further warms up the cell [115,116]. Large battery packs are particularly vulnerable to the dangers of fire and explosion caused by steadily increasing temperatures. So, to make LIBs safer and more reliable, it is important to understand the processes and causes of thermal runaway. Then, you can utilize that knowledge to create useful materials.

Battery thermal management systems (BTMSs) are often considered active or passive. A particular coolant, such as air or liquid, is pushed to circulate in the active system to recover the cells' heat energy [117]. Hydrogels, phase change materials (PCM), and heat pipes are components of a passive system that enhance the overall efficiency of BTMS without requiring power usage. By integrating active and passive components at the same time, hybrid systems may be created. Both methods are used according to the requirements of EVs because they significantly impact the system's cost and efficiency, and each has its own set of pros and cons [118]. An “active system” is a method of cooling or heating that employs external equipment to facilitate the effective dissipation of heat from battery cells. These external devices use power to regulate the battery temperature. Some examples of active BTMS are thermoelectric coolers, pumps for forced liquids, and blowers or fans for forced air. The battery pack became more complicated, expensive, and heavy because of the active BTMS's power consumption and moving components [119].

One energy storage technique that uses lithium-ion batteries as its main component is electrochemical energy storage, which involves converting electrical energy into chemical energy and then storing and releasing that chemical energy as electrical energy [120]. The technological features of a lithium-ion battery include a quick production cycle, a small size, enhanced environmental adaptability, bidirectional control, and a rapid reaction time [121].

There are two primary approaches to controlling the temperature of lithium-ion batteries: active cooling and passive cooling or active and passive composite cooling. One kind of active cooling uses air, while another uses liquid as its cooling medium. Two examples of passive cooling methods are heat pipe cooling and phase change material (PCM) cooling [122,123]. The present research focuses on combining phase change material cooling with various active cooling techniques, the most prevalent of which is air or liquid cooling [124].

To reduce the internal temperature of packaged battery cells, Jin et al. [125] used a forced convection with air technique. The cooling system's performance was measured by experimenting with different air channel layouts and battery cell gap distances. The design was optimized of the battery module to increase its cooling performance. Conducting a sensitivity analysis to see how different design factors affected battery performance was the first step in optimizing the system. An optimisation method was used to ascertain the best channel design based on the analysis's output. The improved air channel design lowered the peak battery cell temperature by 0.883 K and the temperature difference by 1.202 K, representing a small but appreciable improvement in thermal uniformity. These improvements are especially important for enhancing cell longevity and performance balancing in large battery modules.

In order to enhance the li-ion batteries performance by lowering operating temperatures under varying rates of coolant flow, Hasan et al. [126] used computational fluid dynamics (CFD) simulation to investigate the impact of a new design of an effective system for air-cooling. Depending on the operating temperature, li-ion batteries can work better or last shorter. Applying air as a coolant fluid to disperse the heat from li-ion batteries, the spacing between the batteries is explored by flowing the air within the cooling pack. Spacing of 2, 4, and 6 mm was examined, and it is seen that larger spacing can enhance airflow distribution and cooling effectiveness. While the absolute changes in temperature are quite low, they do indicate a trend for more uniform removal of heat when airflow channels are less constricted. Results might range from 15,000 to 30,000 for the Reynolds numbers (Re). The mean Nusselt numbers (Nu) rise as the Re number does. Results show that Nu, the thermal performance, increases with increasing spacing.

Fig. 23 shows the results of an experiment in which Oyewola et al. [127] modified a conventional Z-type BTMS by adding a step-like divergence plenum, which changed the distribution pattern of airflow and increased the efficiency of cooling. Using a proven Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) approach, it was shown that the cooling responses of the BTMS were affected by the duration and number of steps in the plenum of divergence. Findings show that the step amount significantly affects the battery pack's heat dissipation capability, according to analysis of 1, 3, 4, and 7-step BTMS models. With a 30-mm step length, a 7-step case model outperformed the usual Z-type model by 3.94 K regarding the system's highest temperature and 5.93 K regarding cell temperature differential. For every scenario, the results demonstrated that the step length significantly affected the cooling performance. The variations in ∆Tmax values across the four-step models (cases 2, 3, and 4) are small yet significant since they quantify the temperature homogeneity inside a battery pack. As shown in Fig. 24 , the activities of thermal behavior in instance 3 are more uniform at lower flow velocities than in cases 2 and 4. However, as the velocity rises, this uniformity begins to diminish.

By modifying the intake air temperature and the number of cooling fans, Widyantara et al. [128] optimized the lithium-ion battery pack composition of electric vehicles for optimal operating temperatures using an air-cooling method. Lattice Boltzmann method was utilized to develop a computational model of a 2.31 kWh, 74 V battery pack. The result was that adding more cooling fans decreased the temperature difference among the cells within the battery pack, and decreasing ambient air temperature decreased the average temperature inside the battery pack. The thermodynamic control of a liquid-cooled, shell-type battery module experiencing rapid charging and discharging cycles, as well as the thermal runaway phenomenon, was examined by Xu et al. [129]. The finest performance with minimal power consumption was achieved by configuring three cooling fans with an intake air temperature of 25 °C, according to the optimization. The setup managed to keep all the battery cells within the ideal temperature range, even though the largest temperature differential was still 15 °C. Air velocity rose, and temperature reduced as the number of inlets grew, according to the data. Fig. 25 shows that as the number of inlets increased, so did the number of cooling fans.

The battery cells were stacked in a pentagon pattern, and the researcher used faced air convection [130]. Researchers used a computational technique to study the effect of intermediate cell distance (4, 6, and 8 mm), air velocity (0.15, 0.30, and 0.45 m/s), ad air inlet location (60, 75, and 90 mm) on the battery thermal performance. To find the sweet spot for each input parameter, considering every possible output response, a grey relational analysis (GRA) is used. This configuration results in an 8 % drop in the temperature of the peak cell, from 48.32 to 36.83 °C. An airflow improviser is included in the research to investigate how the temperature of the battery cells is affected by its geometrical factors, including the adviser angle, fillet angle, and distance from the module's centre. With an airflow improver in place, the air velocity may be increased from 1.76 to 2.302 m/s (19 %), and the turbulence intensity can be reduced from 196 to 0.350 m2/s2 (33 %). Consequently, by using the pentagonal cell design with an airflow improver and ideal operating settings, the temperature of the cells may be decreased by 8.6 %. Table 2 presents an overview of related studies on air cooling of Li-ion batteries.

Ding et al. [131] simulated the thermal dissipation of a lithium-ion battery pack and determined its thermal distribution by studying liquid cooling systems with various channels. The findings show that the number of channels has an inverse relationship with the dispersion of temperatures for both the maximum and minimum temperatures. The maximum temperature that a square-channel liquid cooling system can reach is lower than that of a circular-channel system. At the same time, the aspect ratio of the rectangular channel is inversely proportional to the maximum temperature. On top of that, the battery pack temperature dispersion first goes down and then up when the aspect ratio becomes up. Additionally, the temperature dispersion for a single li-ion battery will rise first and then gradually fall from the entrance along the coolant flow direction. Wang et al. [132] put forth a unique two-phase liquid immersion system. The researchers examined the battery's cooling capabilities to assess how thermal management impacts the 18,650 LIB's performance. They tested four distinct cooling techniques under varying rates of discharge and dynamic conditions of load: forced and natural convection, single-phase (mineral oil), and two-phase (SF33 fluid). The findings indicate that the cell's temperature remained below 34 °C in all testing settings, and the battery's temperature variation did not surpass 5 °C, showing that immersion cooling of two-phase with SF33 fluid was extremely successful. The boiling point of SF33 absorbed a significant amount of heat during high C discharge from the battery, efficiently maintaining the cell temperature within an ideal range of 33–34 °C.

A liquid-cooled aluminum housing with multiple flow channels accommodates four and a half cylindrical batteries in the module. Compared to charging, the draining process heats the battery so much more. The maximum temperature of the battery module drops slightly with an increase in the coolant flow rate but the temperature difference does not change, leading to a much higher pressure drop. Prevention of thermal propagation pertinent to internal and corner battery thermal runaway was examined with the given liquid-cooled shell. The fact that the temperature of the adjacent battery can be maintained below 70 °C indicates heat dissipation by the surrounding liquid flow plays a great role in suppressing the development of thermal runaway efficiently. As indicated by Fig. 26 , when the peak flow velocity is 1.6 m/s, the battery module and test battery experience a temperature of 85.29 °C, whereas the adjacent cell experiences a temperature of only 44.51 °C.

You et al. [133] in their research analyzed the effect of different operating conditions and cooling system design configurations on the liquid cooling plate for lithium-ion batteries and the heat transfer rate, pumping power, and battery thermal management (BTM) parameters. The battery module temperature reduced from 53.8 °C to 50.7 °C, with an increase in pumping power from 0.036 W to 0.808 W; the research further indicated that the cooling plate flow rate increased from 2 L/min to 6 L/min. The paraffin melting point is 25 °C, and copper foam has a porosity of 98 % and pore size of 20 ppi. As a bonus, the mean battery module temperature was lowered, and the power of pumping was reduced when the number and breadth of the cooling channels were increased. Raising the flow rate was the most important factor in regulating the battery's temperature. Furthermore, the orthogonal analysis found that raising the cooling plate channel number achieved optimal cooling performance and lower pumping power. Following this, a six-channel cooling plate was placed on the bottom and top of the battery module, and the cooling performance was enough to keep the average temperature below 45 °C. Under the rate discharging of 3C- situation, the battery module mean temperature dropped from 53.3 °C to 51.7 °C, as seen in Fig. 27 , as the cooling plate channels number grew from four to six.

Using a suite of numerical models, Yates et al. [134] examined the efficacy of two liquid cooling schemes for lithium-ion battery packs. The two most efficient ideas, a small channel-cooled cylinder (MCC) and a channel-cooled heat sink (CCHS) are used to study the impacts of various parameters such as intake locations, mass flow rate, hole diameter, and channel number. With mass flow rates of more than 5E-05 kg/s, the findings demonstrate that both designs can control the highest temperature to below 313 K, and both systems can control the highest temperature changing to below 3.15 K. The MCC design outperforms the CCHS in terms of maximum temperature and temperature uniformity. Despite having a less uniform temperature, the MCC has a lower maximum temperature than the CCHS. The MCC has lower maximum and lowest temperatures within the four-cell geometry. As seen in Fig. 28 since its heat sink is bigger, the CCHS system requires more energy to increase its temperature from a lower beginning state than the MCC system. Table 3 shows an overview of related studies on liquid cooling of Li-ion batteries.

One study that used cooper foam to improve PCM's thermal conductivity was Zhao et al. [135]. Active liquid cooling was also mixed with CPCM, a composite material of copper foam and paraffin, to enhance the cooling capability. A cooling fluid is pumped via the cooling tube, which is distributed equally throughout the foam of copper. A 3D numerical model of the BTMS was developed utilizing active liquid cooling and PCM by employing ANSYS FLUENT. It was demonstrated how thermal management performance of the BTMS varied with time in terms of velocities of material module and liquid input. By lowering the cell temperature by 14 °C, thermal management using CPCM and liquid cooling became significantly enhanced. At the melting point temperature of 25 °C, the material module's volume temperature was controlled strictly, as shown in Fig. 29 .

The BTMS investigated by Mohammed et al. [136] was a heatsink with multi-phase change materials (PCMs) and fluid channels. It contains eight prismatic cells, each containing nine fluid passages and a PCM on the lateral surface.
