Furthermore, each of the three equal sections of the heatsink containing the PCMs is filled with a different PCM. Temperature homogeneity and cooling performance of the BTMS were thoroughly investigated through the utilization of the battery stack and cooling fluids' compactness. Compared to widening the fluid channel for the same heating sink to battery unit volume ratio, the findings show that enlarging the PCMs' thickness stifled the temperature rise by more than 2.85 K and provided superior temperature homogeneity. At elevated ambient temperature, liquid cooling is more effective than air cooling in lowering the temperatures. The operational expense can be maintained at low levels by carefully controlling the volume ratio of cooling channel to the battery unit.

To minimize the peak temperature attained within the battery cells and to achieve a uniform temperature distribution within the module, Kummitha [137] suggested a novel strategy. The novel battery module design took into account the different heights of the cell holders. These cell holders, as in a tray, contain the battery cells and act as fins that enhance the temperature uniformity of the module. With the same boundary conditions and physical properties, the performance of the new and base battery modules was compared under various discharge conditions. In order to determine the fins' relevance, the temperature contours of each cell were analyzed in the battery module. At 1, 2, and 3C rates, the numerical results showed that the base model reached a maximum of 301.5, 303.9, and 308.8 K, respectively, while the novel model reached 300.6, 302.1, and 304 K. The standard battery model has a temperature gradient of 0.26 °C across cells (Tcell4-Tcell6) in the flow directions for a 1C rate discharge, whereas the innovative battery model has a temperature gradient of just 0.04 °C. Liu et al. [138] investigated a self-organizing fluid flow layout specifically for immersion cooling purposes. The cylindrical battery geometry makes it possible to create a net-like pattern of flow. The utilization of an interdigitated manifold system with a checkerboard pattern is expected to promote both the thermal performance of batteries and immersion cooling fluid distribution. The findings imply that having the inlet and outlet on the same side is increasingly beneficial as the rates of charge and discharge are increased. An approach involving the combination of orthogonal testing and single-factor analysis is recommended to investigate factors influencing this impact. The best model reduces the highest temperature by 12.56 % and the differential in temperature by 43.81 %. With a pressure drop of 8.89 Pa and a 5.844C discharge rate, the temperature difference is just 3.86 K, and the maximum temperature is 305.09 K. The ideal design of a 17-battery module further proves that self-organized immersion cooling significantly enhances battery thermal performance.

The bionic spider model's web channel for heat dissipation in liquids was creatively created by Yao et al. [139]. The first step is to do numerical simulations of the lithium-ion battery pack with a coolant Re of 100 operating in a highest-temperature environment of 40 °C and a 3C melting rate. Secondly, researchers examined the pack temperature distribution of the battery at various discharge rates (1C4C). The temperature differential of the battery pack surpasses 5 °C in the first 600 s of the melting process, necessitating the creation of four distinct coolant flow arrangement schemes; however, this can be better managed towards the conclusion of the melting rate at 3C. Findings demonstrate the existence of a crucial Reynolds number that, when discharged, ensures a maximum temperature of 38.6 °C, fixing the temperature homogeneity of the pack for battery at about 2 °C, and results in a pressure drop of coolant of about 0.78 Pa. Table 4 shows an overview of related studies on active thermal management systems.

Passive cooling strategies have gained significant attention due to their simplicity, low energy consumption, and silent operation. Among them, phase change materials (PCMs) are widely used because of their high latent heat capacity and ability to absorb excess heat during battery operation. Recent studies have demonstrated the effectiveness of form-stable PCMs, composite PCMs, and encapsulated PCMs in managing thermal performance. Babapoor et al. [140] showed that paraffin infused with carbon fiber improved both thermal conductivity and temperature uniformity in 18,650 battery cells, reducing the peak temperature by ∼17 % under 2 W load. Similarly, form-stable CPCMs incorporating polyethylene and expanded graphite have achieved temperature reductions of up to 18 °C during high-rate discharge (10C), while maintaining structural stability. Napa et al. [141] suggested combining PCM with a high matrix of conductive plate metal to overcome the drawbacks of air-cooled systems' heat transfer properties. The uniformity (ΔT) and maximum temperature of the cell (Tmax) are more than the allowed norms of 3 °C and 45 °C, respectively, due to the poor heating transmission capabilities linked with natural convection at high energy and melting rates. Following that, the model is tweaked to examine how PCM affects the increase in cell temperature at various melting rates ranging from 8 A to 25 A (≈1C to 3.2C) and varied temperatures of operation ranging from 25 °C to 35 °C. A greater temperature gradient results from the limited heat dissipation caused by PCM's low thermal conductivity. A new thermal management system (TMS) using a plate metal matrix encircling a printed circuit module (PCM) is suggested to keep the battery module within acceptable operating temperature limits. This system would improve heating conduction by the PCM and boost heat dissipation. Using this method, the maximum temperature of the module throughout operation at 25 A (3.2C) and 35 °C was reduced to 44.1 °C, and the temperature differential was reduced to 1.6 °C.

Two separate heat extraction media types were investigated in a new passive cooling system by Verma et al. [141]: a heat sink based on isothermal storage of PCM and a heating sink based on fined-based enhanced thermal transfer. Understanding the respective contributions of each sub-parts in the extraction of heating and realising the maximal coolant potential feasible from a combined implementation are the goals of investigating these two sub-components in mutually competing and complementary situations. Researchers examined the differences in heat extraction mechanisms caused by broad material qualities like retrofit dimensions and intensive material properties like thermal diffusivity on a case-by-case basis. When the two cooling processes are compared in terms of heat rates, it becomes clear that the fin sub-components and PCM have a ratio of mass of 0.8 and LR of 1, respectively. They work together to produce a maximum cooling of 142.1 W. The suggested passive cooling system has the potential to achieve a maximum temperature decrease of 29.3 K, which is more than enough to keep the EV power unit at an optimal working temperature even in hot climates.

Another notable passive strategy is the integration of heat pipes with PCMs. This configuration passively transfers heat from the cell surface to a remote PCM reservoir, improving heat spreading and preventing local thermal buildup. These systems have demonstrated superior control of maximum temperature while maintaining compact designs, especially under intermittent or pulsed load profiles.

In order to solve the manufacturing and thermal safety problems caused by the complicated electric resistance behaviours in lithium-ion batteries, Kim et al. [142] investigated a new use of heat pipes as passive cooling devices. Using the Equivalent Circuit Model as a foundation, a novel heat generation model was developed, using MATLAB and Simulink to forecast the power consumption of battery packs. To analyze the feasibility of parameters for heating pipes and to predict the availability of cooling by simulation, this complex calculation of the electrochemical method is combined and tested with system-level thermal modeling. Most importantly, the researchers discovered that a heating pipe might bring the difference in temperature between the surrounding environment and the cell down to lower than three degrees in higher output conditions of power, such rates ranging from 4C to 8C.

To passively cool the batteries of electric vehicles, Chen et al. [143] used a composite PCM with form-stable properties. Three different cooling designs are tested using a numerical model in the space between two batteries. The findings demonstrate that a combination of forced air convection and composite PCM may effectively prevent the battery from accumulating heat at 5C, lowering the temperature of the core by 15.9 K. Additionally, by filling the clearance by PCM composite, the temperature can be reduced by 15.7 K. Forced air convection, rather than PCM, is more effective in reducing the rate of increase in battery temperature when the discharge rate is reduced. There is a limit to how high a battery can go when using thicker PCM; however, as the PCM thickness ratio to battery width rises from 0 to 0.1, the highest temperature drops. The composite PCM's heat absorption capacity was significantly enhanced by adding 4.6 wt% graphite and forced air with a velocity of 20 m·s−1 outperformed PCM in terms of cooling behavior. Table 5 presents an overview of related studies on passive thermal management systems.

Despite their benefits, passive systems require careful optimization of PCM melting temperature, geometry, and thermal contact resistance to avoid delayed phase change activation or heat retention after prolonged operation.

Hybrid thermal management systems (HTMS) combine multiple cooling mechanisms—such as PCM with forced air, PCM with liquid cooling, or PCM with heat pipes—to overcome the limitations of standalone systems. These configurations aim to merge the thermal buffering capability of PCMs with the continuous heat removal of convective or conductive techniques.

Zhang et al. [145] demonstrated a PCM–heat pipe–fan system that reduced the cell surface temperature by ∼17 °C under a 5C discharge, while enhancing uniformity and preventing thermal runaway propagation. Similarly, Rao et al. [146] evaluated a PCM–copper foam–liquid setup that resulted in a 31.4 % peak temperature drop and reduced ΔT to under 3 °C during aggressive cycling conditions. A hybrid cooling system was compared to active and passive cooling by Isfahani et al. [147]. The system consists of PCM and microchannels that are supported by metal foam. Results indicated that passive BTMS, such as PCM/metal foam, leads to a more uniform temperature distribution and lower temperature difference (approximately 1 K), but it has a higher maximum temperature (approximately 319 K). However, active BTMS, which includes microchannels, results in a larger ∆T (about 9 K) and a lower Tmax (approximately 312 K). The maximum and minimum temperatures are kept within reasonable limits by applying the microchannels and PCM/metal foam hybrid system. Here, the highest temperature reaches 308.4 K, and the differential in temperature is around 3.4 K.

The novel hybrid nano/dielectric fluid cooling system statistically examined by Tousi et al. [148] used indirect and direct coolant cooling procedures. The Al2O3-water nanofluid and HFE-7100 mediums were chosen for the direct and indirect cooling systems. The impact of several energy-related elements on the temperature behavior of the LIB packs was further understood by comparing 21,700 with the new LIB model, 4680-types. Numerical studies show that when used as coolants for indirect cooling channels, 4 % VF Alumina nanofluid with an inlet flow rate of 0.3 m/s outperforms water with an inlet flow rate of 0.2 m/s in terms of thermal performance. As the results of the investigation indicate, curved cooling channels with separator plates reduced the LIB bundle's temperature non-homogeneity by 0.16 °C and its maximum temperature by 0.61 °C. Two LIB designs with different dimensions, i.e., 21700 and 4680, are compared with a cooling system with an equivalent number of cells.

Faizan et al. [149] proposed a novel thermal management concept for pouch cell lithium-ion batteries. The proposed system incorporates liquid cooling and phase change materials. A hybrid Al2O3-Cu-water nanofluid flows in the system through cold plates designed with converging twisted serpentine mini channels (CTSCs). At the high discharge rate of 8C, the results indicated that the module temperature is maintained within acceptable levels due to the application of hybrid cooling. The cooling performance is enhanced compared to the uniform serpentine channel when cold plates are grooved with CTSC. At m = 0.0005 kg/s and φ = 1 %, the square cross-section has the minimum pressure drop, and the kite cross-section has lower Tmax (328.05 K) and ΔT (5.65 K) compared to others. The maximum discharge current (Tmax) of the battery drops as m rises, and the ΔT of every cell is much lower than 3.5 K. Tmax is reduced by 6.24 K, and ΔT is reduced by 0.69 K as a result of 5 % hybrid nanoparticle dispersion, respectively. Using heat insulation materials, phase change cooling, and nanofluid cooling, Ouyang et al. [150] developed a method for managing the thermal state of batteries that is both efficient and energy-saving. First, the electrochemical, thermal, and fluid models are integrated to form the system model, and then the model's validity is examined. Scheme 6 successfully prevents the spread of thermal runaway and lowers the maximum battery temperature from 1013.50 K to 328.34 K under typical heat dissipation conditions. Even under the most severe circumstances, Scheme 6 manages to lower the maximum battery temperature to 380.34 K, and the system's heat may be gradually removed. Lastly, the regression equation and optimal combination factors are established using a uniform-precision rotatable central composite design to further increase the cooling performance. Compared to plan 6, the enhanced plan achieves a 23 % reduction in maximum battery temperature and a 22 % reduction in economic index.

Mashayekhi et al. [151] studied the temperature response in li-ion batteries under high melting rates using a novel TMS that combines active and passive approaches. The passive component of TMS was refined paraffin in a block shape with porous foam of copper metal, while the active component was an aluminum mini-channel that contained coolant flow. The results demonstrated that the maximum temperature drops with increasing flow rates across all three Reynold numbers tested using active and hybrid techniques. Additionally, studies have shown that passive cooling is ineffective in reducing high discharge rates to a level where the battery temperature stays below the 60 °C safety limit. At a Reynolds number of 340 and a heating generating power of 3.7 W, the hybrid thermal management system lowered the battery steady-state temperature by 19.5 % compared to the active technique. In active approaches, nanofluids may lower the highest battery temperature by 15.5 % compared to the standard case; in hybrid methods, the reduction is 8.5 %. Table 6 shows an overview of related studies on hybrid techniques.

The key strength of hybrid systems lies in their scalability and adaptability to high-power applications such as electric vehicles. They address the inherent drawback of PCMs—low thermal conductivity—by ensuring active heat extraction once the PCM saturates. However, these systems introduce design complexity, cost, and integration challenges, especially concerning compactness, reliability, and material compatibility. To fully exploit hybrid systems' potential, future research must focus on modular design, control algorithms, and smart switching between active and passive modes based on real-time thermal loads.

In contrast to the conventional thermal management techniques like air cooling, liquid cooling, and hybrid cooling, the usage of phase change materials (PCMs) is a prominent and encouraging approach as an active/ passive/ hybrid thermal management of batteries that can help to limit battery temperature within an ideal temperature without thermal runaway. PCM uses no energy and does not require any additional electro-mechanical parts [20,152,153]. During the phase change process, PCM absorbs or releases a significant quantity of latent heat energy while the temperature remains almost constant. As a result, battery temperature uniformity is maintained, and it helps retain the battery temperature within a suitable range, accompanied by a higher latent heat of phase transition and a smaller temperature and volume change [154,155]. In recent years, the application of PCM for BTMS has been less expensive due to technological advancements. PCM-based BTMS is the preferred approach for battery temperature regulation within safe operational limits. PCM-based BTMS is also preferred because of lower initial and maintenance costs, higher integration potential, efficiency, and life cycle.

An extensive volume of research articles has been published on the BTMs using PCMs. Phase change fluid (PCF) as a heat transfer medium and encapsulated solid PCM wrapped in the battery pack for heat transport are the two main ways PCM is used in the Li-ion BTMs. The constraints of the conventional PCM in the application of battery thermal management have been broken by the advent of novel PCMs like PCF and flexible phase change materials (FPCM) [156,157]. Fig. 30 illustrates the range of applications of PCM-based BTMs in different scales [158]. Table 7 illustrates that the number of PCM-based BTM studies has been rising yearly, and the annual review keywords are summarised. This summary shows that in most earlier studies, low-temperature settings and PCM in conjunction with other thermal management solutions were left out, and Li-ion battery thermal management in a high-temperature environment has been focused. However, in-depth study on power battery thermal management, including PCM-cooled and PCM-heated BTMs is less attended.

However, most of the earlier review articles on BTM techniques are incomplete and have limitations. Traditional thermal management techniques are insufficient to meet advanced power batteries' desired thermal management [159,160]. As a result, combined PCM and BTM techniques have been developed, and extensive research is needed for further development [161,162]. It worth to mention that Pure PCMs refer to unmodified phase change materials (such as paraffin waxes or fatty acids) that rely solely on their intrinsic latent heat capacity to regulate temperature. While they offer simplicity and high energy storage density, their low thermal conductivity often limits heat dissipation efficiency. To address this limitation, nano-enhanced PCMs incorporate thermally conductive nanoparticles (e.g., Al₂O₃, CuO, graphene) into the PCM matrix, significantly improving thermal conductivity and response time without compromising storage capacity. In contrast, hybrid techniques involve integrating PCMs with active cooling components (such as liquid cooling plates, heat pipes, or forced-air systems) to achieve higher heat removal rates and dynamic thermal control. These hybrid systems often combine the passive benefits of PCMs with the responsiveness of active methods, offering superior performance under variable operating conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characteristics and types of phase change materials
ce:section.ce:para: To limit the storage unit size to a compact size and an easy-to-design container, PCMs should have a higher density, minimal volume changes between the solid and liquid phases, and lower vapor pressure at the working temperature. Also, the application of PCM depends on the melting characteristics of PCM and the segregation of solids, which may gradually cause decrement in storage capacity. One of the important characteristics of the PCM for the BTMs is the appropriate crystallization rate, which is one of the material's fundamental properties, along with very small subcooling. Furthermore, an increase in a few degrees of subcooling will hinder, if not wholly preclude the heat extraction from the battery surface. To ensure a longer lifespan of the storage unit, the chosen PCM must be chemically stable because it may experience oxidative deterioration, chemical breakdown, or incompatibility with the container of the PCM storage. For safety reasons, the PCM should not be poisonous, flammable, or explosive [164,165].

The material's phase change temperature must match or be very near to the operating temperature during selecting a PCM for a particular application. In particular, the latent heat should be as high as possible on a volumetric scale to decrease the storage unit's size. Additionally, PCM with higher specific heat is desirable for increasing the sensible storage capacity. In both the solid and liquid states of the PCM, higher thermal conductivity is preferred to improve the energy charging and discharging processes and achieve a more steady temperature distribution within the energy storage device [21,166,167].

Based on the intended application, PCM has different initial states—liquid-gas, solid-gas, solid-solid, and solid-liquid. In general, the heat storage capacity for the PCM is significantly higher than that of any conventional material (for a 15 °C temperature difference), such as water, wood, plastic, and others. Among all types, the solid-liquid PCM is one of the most popular latent heat energy storage systems, especially for the BTMs in the automotive industry [168,169] and exhibits the greatest potential. Other types of PCM, like liquid-gas, solid-gas, and solid-solid, are not so popular with BTMs due to their operational restrictions. Three primary groups of PCMs may be distinguished: organic, inorganic, and eutectic. Generally, Organic PCM has a greater temperature tolerance and higher replacement rate, although volume changes are negligible. The development of organic PCM with the potential for supercooling and viability from an economic standpoint is receiving increased attention. Because of their more affordable price, a wider range of melting temperature, thermo-chemical stabilities, and transitional phase change enthalpies, organic PCMs have drawn greater interest than inorganic PCMs. The prevalent drawback of organic PCMs is that their installation costs are often higher than those of inorganic PCMs [168].

Most PCMs have a lower thermal conductivity (k), which has been a cause for serious concern and restricts the application of PCM for the BTMs. Numerous investigations have been conducted to boost the thermally conductive components of PCMs by using carbon fiber (CF), metal-based foam, and carbon allotrope (graphite) [140]. The composite material increases the k value significantly [169]. Water, liquid solutions, hydrated salts, and molten salt are examples of inorganic PCMs [170], whereas alkane, alcohol, paraffin, and organic acid are examples of organic PCMs. Liquid-gas PCMs include water and liquid organic compounds; the most commonly used paraffin wax is a solid-liquid PCM [171]. Leakage of the melted PCM is a common issue that restricts its use as a thermoelectric storage material (TES). Shape-stabilized PCMs are developed to solve the leakage issue of the PCMs based on the microstructure packing technique [172]. On the other hand, shape-stabilized PCMs usually exhibit poor mechanical properties, and their thermal resistance is increased by their poor alignment with heat transfer devices. Superior mechanical capabilities of flexible phase change materials (FPCMs) have gained significance with the development of wearable and flexible electronic gadgets. There are many uses for flexible phase-change materials (FPCMs), such as temperature control and thermal energy storage [173].

Due to the widespread use of LIBs, the operation of batteries is quite complex and challenging, considering a wide range of ambient temperatures ranging from sub-zero to tens of degrees Celsius. Generally, it is supposed the performance of LIBs is superior when the operational temperatures are within 20 to 45 °C. Too high or too low a temperature may harm batteries and affect thermal and electrochemical characteristics. The impact of battery heat generation at different operational temperatures can be easily understood in Fig. 31 . Electrochemical action reduces significantly at the lower ambient temperature, increasing electrolyte viscosity and internal resistance. During the expected average operating temperatures of 20 to 45 °C, the LIBs operate safely, heat generation is also moderate, and the heating rate is <10 °C per minute. Conversely, thermal abuse occurs when the battery temperature is about 90 °C or more, and the SEI film decomposes. In this situation, a series of side reactions initiates, including high exothermic reactions of organic solvent reactions, separator melting, and cathode materials decomposition [174]. This eventuality leads to higher heat generation, and the electrolyte may burn aggressively, and explosions may even happen when large amounts of toxic gas are released. Furthermore, the fire of one cell can propagate to another, thus causing battery pack fire and massive disaster. Therefore, control of battery cell temperature is of utmost necessity from a safety point of view, and proper battery thermal management (BTMs) is necessary. The fundamental objective of a BTMS is to maintain the battery operation under a uniform distribution throughout the cell and pack [175].

Several researchers have explored the utilization of PCM control of battery thermal management. The PCMs had the potential to store a significant volume of latent heat, which kept the temperature of the battery pack primarily stable. A numerical model considering PCM-based BTMs was developed by Shen et al. [176]. Their research revealed that PCM has a good capacity for storing heat energy and homogenizing the temperature because it can effectively lower the maximum temperature of the batteries and the maximum temperature difference during the discharge process. However, due to the inadequate thermal conductivity, pure PCM is vulnerable to absorbing excessive heat and changes in the local temperature of the battery pack during the high-rate charging and discharging process. Therefore, enhancing PCM's thermal conductivity is one of the major areas of further research for the appropriate BTMs. In recent years, many researchers have attempted to explore the relevance of PCMs in connection with the BTMs [177,178].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermal modeling of Li-ion batteries with PCMs
ce:section.ce:para: For a proper understanding of the characteristics of PCM-based thermal management systems for Li-ion batteries, mathematical modeling is a vital tool before finalizing the actual design. Such a modeling approach offers insight into the system's thermal behavior under various operating conditions, which is of utmost importance for designing and optimizing a PCM-based thermal management system for Li-ion battery modules. The physical processes within the system are described using the energy balance through the heat transfer equations, which represent how heat moves through the system to the battery's surface area exposed to PCM and the surrounding air.

Numerous techniques based on numerical simulations and experiments have been developed to explore battery thermal behavior and thermal management systems. However, numerical/ mathematical modeling is the most sophisticated technique for conducting initial analysis on the BTMs before the expensive actual design. Stefan et al. [179] first introduced a phase-change model addressing the challenge of predicting phase-change system behavior due to the non-linear atmosphere of the moving interfaces (liquid/solid or solid/solid). This study proposes several thermal models for the heat transmission mechanism in PCMs within a battery cell or system.

Mathematical modeling plays a crucial role in predicting and optimizing the thermal behavior of PCM-integrated battery systems. Modeling approaches generally fall into three categories: lumped-parameter models, finite difference/finite element models, and CFD-based models. Lumped models simplify the system into discrete thermal nodes and use energy balance equations (e.g., Q = mc ∆ T ) to predict temperature evolution. They are computationally efficient but neglect spatial temperature gradients and phase change dynamics. Finite element or finite volume models improve spatial resolution by solving the transient heat conduction equation:

where ρ is density, Cp is specific heat, k is thermal conductivity, and Qgen is internal heat generation. In PCM applications, an enthalpy method is commonly used to model phase change by incorporating latent heat into the energy term. CFD-based models further enhance detail by coupling the heat transfer equations with fluid flow equations (e.g., Navier–Stokes) to account for natural convection in molten PCM. However, CFD models are computationally expensive and require complex meshing and convergence schemes.

Common modeling challenges include handling the nonlinearity of PCM phase change, sedimentation in nano-PCM, and geometry-dependent heat transfer paths in battery modules. Additionally, model accuracy often relies on assumptions such as constant properties, perfect PCM–cell contact, or uniform heat generation, none of which hold in practice. To improve predictive reliability, hybrid multi-scale models that couple electrochemical behavior, heat generation, and phase change are gaining traction in recent literature.

In general, the heat generation inside a Li-ion battery is due to several factors, such as electrochemical reaction, ohmic internal resistance, polarization, electrolyte decomposition, and solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) film decomposition. However, considering all these factors, accurately modeling the Li-ion battery is challenging. The generalized basic governing Eqs. [180] are given as

Bernardi et al. [181] first proposed a general equation for the heat generation and rejection in the battery cells. After that, several researchers modified the governing equations considering the volumetric heat generation in a cell and taking care of the reversible and irreversible heat transfer for the PCM-coupled battery systems that account for latent heat and heat generation:

The two primary numerical approaches to solving the heat generation model of PCM-based BTM are the enthalpy porosity and temperature methods. The energy equation (temperature field) is solved for the solid and liquid domains using a single equation using the enthalpy porosity approach. This technique allows us to know the solid-liquid interface condition and provides information about the transient phenomena of the mushy zone between the two phases. Such an approach corresponds to the simplest approach to solving the phase-change process. However, the meshing effort is significant in solving the mushy zone.

The energy conservation equations during the phase-change process (melting or freezing) are defined as [182]:

For the optimum design of battery thermal management systems, several researchers have attempted to study the BTMs. Paraffin is one of the most commonly used PCMs due to its lower cost, lack of separation of phases, feasible enthalpy, and changeable phase-transition temperature with different compositions. In order to analyze the performance of the thermal management system, Wang et al. [183] conducted a numerical modeling of PCM-based batteries. They observed that PCM can effectively reduce the extreme temperature and highest temperature difference of the battery during the complete discharge of the battery; they concluded that PCM has the potential to store energy capacity and the ability to homogenize temperature. However, due to the high charging and discharging rate, pure PCM is susceptible to excessive heat accumulation and local temperature rise of the battery pack due to its lower thermal conductivity. Therefore, the question of improved thermal conductivity of PCM is rising daily. Akkurt et al. [184] investigated the cooling performance of a lithium-ion battery pack with three cylindrical cells in a channel with laminar airflow surrounding it. The temperature-lowering tendency improves with increasing time when the speed is increased from 0.1 to 0.3 cm/s. The battery pack's lowest temperature was observed at a battery spacing of 10 cm. A 10-cm battery gap lowered the right-side battery's temperature.

The modern trend in BTMs is hybrid cooling methods that combine PCM and liquid. During PCM solidification, liquid cooling predominates because of the liquid's superior thermal conductivity over air. According to a numerical study by Kong et al. [185], a hybrid PCM combined with BTMs that use liquid cooling dramatically lowers the needless power used for liquid cooling while simultaneously improving the battery pack's thermal performance throughout the cycle at various ambient temperatures. A unique conjugate cooling structure utilizing PCM and liquid cooling technologies was proposed by Song et al. [186]. Their study reported that conjugate cooling considerably lowered the steady-state cell temperature, and the rate of temperature rise compared to a single PCM or liquid cooling environment. A modern hybrid BTM based on PCM and liquid cooling was proposed by Zhang et al. [187]. They observed that heat transfer is under control even in the most demanding operating situations of the batteries by combining the benefits of PCM with a liquid thermal management scheme. The latent heat of the PCM in this system is primarily responsible for maintaining the battery's operational temperature. The majority of the PCM's latent heat is wasted when a single cell goes out of control. At this stage, the liquid cooling starts to function. Heat is removed via the cooling channel and the aluminum plate, which lowers heat transfer to nearby cells and prevents runaway heat transmission. A numerical model for PCM-based cylindrical battery cooling was created by Yang et al. [188] and validated through experimental imaging of PCM melting around battery cells. They investigated two kinds of acrylic and aluminum battery pack housing enclosures. Because of PCM adherence to the acrylic, it was discovered that metal housing was a better option for heat management and that PCM melted more slowly inside the acrylic housing.

Modeling the complex thermal behavior of PCM-based BTMS presents several challenges, primarily due to the transient and nonlinear nature of melting, solidification, and nanoparticle transport phenomena. The enthalpy–porosity method is one of the most widely used techniques for simulating the phase change process, where the mushy region between solid and liquid states is treated as a porous medium, and the liquid fraction governs the effective thermal properties. This approach allows accurate tracking of latent heat absorption and release without explicitly deforming the computational grid. For nano-enhanced PCMs, multi-phase mixture models or Eulerian–Eulerian frameworks are commonly adopted to capture the effects of nanoparticle sedimentation, Brownian motion, and thermophoresis, which can alter local conductivity and viscosity. Furthermore, coupled CFD–electrochemical models are increasingly employed to simultaneously simulate internal heat generation, electrolyte behavior, and PCM response under realistic battery operation. These methods collectively improve the fidelity of numerical predictions and help overcome the traditional limitations associated with purely conductive or lumped-parameter models in PCM-based BTMS simulations.

Several researchers have explored the different approaches of BTMs applying PCM, which are reported in refs. [126,161] and others. A summary of the various problems on PCM-based BTMS using computation solutions is summarised in Table 8 .

A significant volume of experimental investigation on PCM-based BTMs is available in the open literature. A novel BTMS based on PCM was developed by Lazrak et al. [194], who also employed copper grids to enhance PCM heat dissipation. The PCM has two plates made of the same material on opposing sides, and ventilation was provided by a fan and battery cells. Hekmat et al. [195] suggested a hybrid BTM for Li-ion batteries with big-capacity prismatic batteries that combine PCM with a traditional cooling system. The maximum temperature will be much lower than under other circumstances when cooling water pipes are embedded into PCM and a hybrid BTM is constructed, demonstrating the advantages of the hybrid BTMs. Considering heatpipe cooling, Huang et al. [196] investigated a PCM/HP system's temperature control. They observed that the PCM/HP modules' temperature could be maintained below 50 °C and that the HP was essential to the PCM's heat transmission and temperature stability. Phase change material (PCM) and bottom liquid cooling techniques were used in a hybrid thermal management system (TMS) for a large-sized power battery module that Zhang et al. [197] experimentally studied. The results show that at cyclic discharge rates of more than 5C, hybrid cooling maintains the temperature differential below 3.5 °C and the maximum battery pack temperature below 50 °C. In an experimental study, Khateeb et al. [198] examined several heat dissipation methods by testing a built Li-ion battery module for the scooter at various C-rates in a laboratory. It was observed that, in comparison to no thermal management case, the use of aluminum foam with PCM leads to a significant temperature decrease of about 50 %.

Furthermore, it guarantees even temperature distribution across the battery module, essential for efficiently functioning the used cells. To compare the cooling capabilities of various combinations of PCM, fins, and air-cooling systems for superior BTM design, Chen et al. [199] carried out several experimental studies. They found that fins or fans produce forced convection of the melted liquid PCM and air when the room temperature climbs above the PCM melting point. Combining a different combination of cooling techniques at 40 °C, 45.1 °C, and 50.7 °C, as well as at room temperatures of 25 °C, 32 °C, and 40 °C during the battery discharge process, the maximum temperature on the battery surfaces is well regulated. Moreover, the maximum temperature variations were merely 2.3 °C, 2.2 °C, and 1.9 °C, well below the average necessity of 5 °C. The effects of vibration on PCM-based BTMS have been carried out by Joshy et al. [200] for the range of vibration of an amplitude of 30–50 mm/s at frequencies between 20 and 50 Hz. They found that battery cells located in the centers and on the corners respond differently to the vibration. The findings demonstrated that when vibration frequency increased, so did the melting rate, which decreased PCM's ability to absorb heat from battery cells. From an experimental study, Qin et al. [201] explored a novel hybrid BTM combining air-forced convection with PCM. They observed that the maximum temperature of the battery and the temperature difference below 45 °C and 5 °C, respectively, at a high discharge rate of 4C. A further volume of research on PCM-based BTMs is well documented in refs. [202,203]. A detailed summary of the PCM-based BTMs – experimental study is presented in Table 9 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Integration of PCMs in Li-ion battery systems
ce:section.ce:para: The efficiency and safety of lithium-ion batteries are greatly influenced by thermal conditions. To address this problem, researchers are adding Phase Change Materials (PCMs) to lithium-ion battery systems. Phase Change Materials (PCMs) possess a great attribute: they can absorb or release significant amounts of thermal energy when they undergo the solid-to-liquid phase change. This addition serves as a thermal buffer for the battery system. During charging or when the computer is operating high-power programs that make it overheat, the phase change materials (PCMs) take in the surplus energy and go through a liquid state. This is utilized for shielding the battery from excessive heating exposure. Conversely, when the temperature is cold outside, the phase change materials (PCMs) become solid and release the heat they have absorbed in an effort to keep the battery at its optimum temperature level. This characteristic guarantees the maintenance of battery integrity and enhancement of functionality, regardless of ambient conditions [210].

Successful integration of phase change materials requires careful design considerations. First, choosing phase change materials (PCMs) whose transition temperature matches the desired operating range of the battery is important. Additionally, the placement of phase change materials (PCMs) in the battery pack is essential. The battery cells and PCMs need to be placed in a way that heat transfer is maximized. Finally, the detailed design must take into account how phase change materials (PCMs) affect factors like weight, volume, and any constraints that will be imposed by the enclosure of the battery [211].

Controlling the lithium-ion batteries' temperature is essential and should be marinated between 20 °C and 50 °C during the charging and discharging process to avoid reduction in life, performance, and thermal flushing. Overheating a battery can cause safety problems such as flaming and explosion [212]. As a passive cooling technique, PCM has been shown to be capable of maintaining the module temperature within its operational range. Researchers incorporate PCM in different ways to develop a very efficient BTMS. In this section, different incorporating ways of PCM in BTMS will be discussed, different work in this field will be summarised and discussed, and the main conclusions will be drawn.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Using of pure PCM for BTMS
ce:section.ce:para: Due to its high latent heat property, PCM can store large amounts of heat. It melts when the battery's temperature exceeds the incorporated PCM's melting point. During melting, the heat released from the battery module is absorbed by the PCM. Many studies explored the impact of incorporating pure PCM numerically and experimentally.

Numerical research offers several benefits compared to experimental research, including time and financial savings. Moreover, the usage of a numerical method enables researchers to test multiple factors that would be challenging to manipulate experimentally. By modeling different factors, researchers can draw general conclusions and gain a deeper understanding of the main processes and cooling events. Lamrani et al. investigated numerically how different kinds of PCM affect the temperature of lithium-ion battery packs. They introduced a simplified model based on the energy balance of the PCM and the battery combined with a heat source. The maximum relative error of the proposed model was 6 % compared to the experimental data from the literature. Four different types of PCM with four different melting temperatures and latent heat, which are RT-31, A-32H, RT-35HC, and A-36H, are considered for this study to investigate their effect on the temperature of the battery module. Results showed that the most suitable PCM is A-32H due to its appropriate melting point and high latent heat of fusion, which can reduce the battery temperature by 3 °C [210]. To implement an efficient BTMS with minimal cost and energy consumption, Wang et al. carried out a numerical investigation on the integration of paraffin wax. The paraffin wax had a melting temperature of 312.15 K and a latent heat of fusion of 213 KJ/kg. This integration was performed with a 10 Ah battery. The authors explored the impact of various parameters, including thermal conductivity, viscosity, and PCM latent heat, as well as the PCM's thickness and the unit shell's thermal conductivity. The results demonstrated that the proposed BTMS can lower the maximum temperature by 7.26 K [183]. Due to the importance of PCM thickness in BTMS, it can enhance heat transfer and thermal management; however, using the large thickness of PCM can increase the size and weight of the battery and may affect the thermal management process. Javani et al. conducted a numerical investigation of PCM thickness on heat transfer and thermal management of a Li-ion battery using the finite volume method as well. A prismatic Li-ion battery cell with dimensions of 146x194x5.4 mm was considered for the study. Various thicknesses of the PCM layers were experimented with, namely 3, 6, 9, and 12 mm. The result indicated that a 12-mm PCM thickness can reduce the battery temperature by 3 K [213]. Bais et al. examined the influence of the thickness of PCM on the temperature of an 18,650 Li-ion battery subjected to a 3C discharge rate. RT42 with thicknesses ranging from 1 mm to 7 mm was employed in the research. The result showed the optimum PCM thickness is 4 mm, which had the capability to maintain the battery temperature below 50 °C [214].

Sevugan et al. compared the performance of two PCMs with different thermo-physical properties: n-docosane and sodium thiosulfate pentahydrate. The research entailed the optimization of PCM thickness for the two types at discharge levels of 5C and 8C. Results showed that the optimum thickness for n-docosane and sodium thiosulfate pentahydrate is 2 mm and 1 mm, respectively, effectively maintaining the cell temperature at 321.3 K and 321.5 K [215]. Jilte et al. optimized the best configuration for BTMS integrated with PCM. The BTMS thermal performance was explored at varying configurations, discharge rates, and ambient conditions. It was found that integrating confined PCM around each cell performs the best in dissipating heat into the ambient and ensuring temperature uniformity of the battery [216]. To reduce the computational time, Ling et al. proposed a 2D thermal network for the BTMS integrated with PCM. The proposed model is compact and can save 99 % of computational time compared to conventional numerical models with an average prediction error of less than 1 K [217]. Lazark et al. conducted a comprehensive study that coupled experimental and numerical approaches to explore the viability of implementing PCM as a passive battery cooling technique. There were two models created in the study. The first, a 1D analysis, was employed to analyze the thermo-physical behavior of PCM and how it influences the Battery BTMS. The second model, a 3D simulation, was created to optimize the BTMS design. The PCM employed in the research was 35 °C and had a latent heat of 157 kJ/kg. In addition, the authors utilized a small-scale experimental system to validate the proposed design. Findings indicated that the new design reduced the system temperature by 5 °C relative to a non-cooled system [194]. Chen et al. conducted a study comparing active and passive cooling methods for reducing the temperature of Li-ion batteries with forced air and PCM, respectively. The study employed 1D and 2D thermal models to simulate the temperature of a system consisting of 16 cylindrical Li-ion battery cells 26,650. Various parameters, including ambient temperature, air velocity, and PCM temperature, were examined, such as paraffin PCM with melting points ranging from 35 °C to 55 °C and a constant latent heat of 181 kJ/kg. Inlet air velocity varied between 0 and 8 m/s across different ambient conditions (0 °C, 10 °C, 20 °C, 30 °C, 40 °C, and 50 °C). The study yielded diverse results and conclusions. While active air cooling demonstrated superior cooling efficiency at high ambient temperatures, the PCM configuration exhibited better temperature uniformity [218].

Although there are various advantages to the numerical approach over the experimental approach, The output results in real life may vary. Specific parameters constrain numerical approaches and may not account for all variables present in the real world. Duan et al. experimentally investigated the effect of adding PCM to the battery to enhance heat transfer and reduce temperature. An electric heater is employed in this experiment to mimic the battery heat generation. Two different configurations are considered in this experiment. The first configuration is a PCM cylinder around the heater; the other configuration is PCM jackets covering the heater. Results showed that the two configurations perform effectively in maintaining the temperature within the defined range [189]. For the optimum battery performance in an electric vehicle, the temperature of the battery cell should be in a specified range.

Various studies have investigated the impact of PCM thickness on the thermal performance of lithium-ion battery packs, but their conclusions vary considerably due to differences in experimental conditions and system configurations. For instance, Zhou et al. experimentally studied the effect of PCM type and thickness on the cooling efficiency and thermal performance of prismatic lithium-ion batteries. For this study, three types of PCM are used: paraffin with melting points of 40,50, and 60 with latent heat of 200, 220, and 220 kJ/kg, respectively. The findings indicate that the reduction of the PCM's melting point can enhance the cooling process. Further, the PCM thickness is critical, with the cooling efficiency reducing when the thickness exceeds 25 mm. Other findings indicate that 25 mm PCM thickness can effectively maintain the maximum temperature of the battery at below 55 °C under various cycles [219]. In contrast, Weng et al. experimentally explore the impact of PCM thickness on the thermal management of a cylindrical Lithium-ion battery. Three different types of PCM with different melting points are considered in this experiment. Three different thicknesses of 5,10 and 15 mm are experimentally investigated. The authors optimized different parameters to obtain the optimal cooling method. Results showed that 10 mm is the optimal thickness for the PCM; moreover, it was found that increasing the time spent laying aside can enhance cooling efficiency [191]. Similarly, Kizilel et al. [220] demonstrated that even a thin layer of pure paraffin (∼3–4 mm) could effectively suppress temperature rise during fast discharges (3C), owing to its high latent heat and proximity to the cell surface. This suggests that thinner PCM layers may suffice when the material is properly integrated with cell geometry and operated under controlled thermal environments.

These conflicting results highlight that optimal PCM thickness is not universal but instead highly dependent on factors such as battery type (cylindrical vs. prismatic), PCM thermal properties (pure vs. composite), discharge rates, and ambient conditions.

Lithium batteries are usually integrated into machines and electric vehicles, where they may be exposed to intermittent or constant vibrations. These vibrations can influence the behavior of BTMS. Therefore, it is necessary to investigate and consider the impact of vibrations on BTMS to have a comprehensive understanding of their working dynamics. Joshy et al. experimentally investigated the effect of vibration on the PCM inside the BTMS. In this study, RT35 is used to sustain the module temperature in the appropriate temperature range of 25 °C to 40 °C. The modules were simulated using an aluminum cylinder containing a ceramic cartridge. Different frequencies and amplitudes were attempted, ranging from 20 to 30 Hz and 30–50 mm/s respectively. Results showed that the battery temperature at the surface rises with increasing frequency and amplitude [200]. It is necessary to select proper PCM properties for BTMS. Sun et al. prepared a eutectic PCM with stable supercooling through the mixture of sodium thiosulfate pentahydrate sodium acetate trihydrate and deionized water for BTMS. Melting point and enthalpy for sodium thiosulfate pentahydrate and sodium acetate trihydrate are 58 °C, 48.5 °C, 248 KJ/kg, and 206 KJ/kg, respectively. The thermophysical properties of the new eutectic PCM were altered, and its final melting point and latent enthalpy became 41.4 °C and 169.29 °C, respectively. The new eutectic PCM processes stable supercooling over 61 °C and has a recovery temperature of 25.4 °C. The authors applied the eutectic PCM to 36 pieces of 18,650 power batteries with a volume ratio of 45 %. Results showed that the discharge capacity improved by 6.8 % in cold conditions at −20 °C [221].

Karimi et al. investigated three different BTMS configurations. The first one is BTMS without PCM, the second one has PCM and the third has PCM surrounded by aluminum foil. The maximum module temperature was calculated using a 1D model using AMEsim software and a 3D model using COMSOL software. The authors prepared an experimental setup, and the results were validated using the 1D and 3D models. Results showed that using PCM and PCM with aluminum foil can lower the temperature of the battery system to reach a minimum of 41.6 °C and 36.2 °C respectively [222].

Different studies also investigated the stabilized form of PCM for BTMS. Wang et al. used graphene oxide for cooling the battery [223]. This type of prepared composite has a high potential to be used in different applications such as cooling photovoltaic [224] or inside the soil in the greenhouse to control the microclimatic conditions for the plants [225]. Rajan et al. examined the application of using 1-Tetradecanol PCM on the thermal performance of cylindrical Li-ion batteries. Using a numerical analysis, the authors concluded that pure PCMs are not perfect for passive cooling the system due to their low thermal conductivity, which should be enhanced by adding thermal conductivity enhancer materials [226]. Although PCM has high latent heat properties and potential in BTMS, its low thermal conductivity may affect the total heat transfer process. Several approaches to improve the thermal conductivity of the PCM include dispersing high thermal conductivity nanoparticles, adding fins and metal foams, or using a hybrid combination of active and passive cooling. The following sections will delve into these topics.

A noticeable variation exists among reported optimal PCM thicknesses, with studies identifying values between 10 mm and 25 mm. This inconsistency can be attributed to several interrelated factors. First, the geometry and type of battery cells significantly influence heat generation and dissipation pathways. Cylindrical cells tend to produce localized hotspots along the core, requiring thicker PCM layers for adequate lateral heat spreading, while prismatic cells with larger surface areas benefit from thinner PCM coatings due to their more uniform heat distribution. Second, discharge rate plays a crucial role—high C-rate operations intensify heat generation, necessitating thicker PCM layers to absorb transient thermal spikes, whereas lower C-rates permit thinner layers to suffice. Third, the thermal properties of the PCM itself critically affect performance: materials with high thermal conductivity and low latent heat (e.g., metal oxide composites) optimize temperature control at smaller thicknesses, whereas pure paraffin-based PCMs with lower conductivity require thicker layers for effective energy absorption. Finally, ambient temperature and airflow conditions further modulate PCM performance; higher ambient temperatures diminish the temperature gradient and slow heat transfer, thus favoring thicker PCM layers. Collectively, these factors explain the apparent contradictions across studies and underscore the importance of context-specific optimization when determining PCM thickness for reliable BTMS design.
Table 10 summarizes some of the work mentioned in this section with its significant findings and highlights critical comments, gaps, and statements.

Any particle less than 100 nm in size is called a nanoparticle. Nanoparticles attracted research attention since their chemical, physical, and biological properties highly differ from macroscale materials [227]. Nanoparticles can be used in many applications to enhance the properties of the base fluid, especially thermal conductivity. Karimi et al. experimentally investigated the effect of NEPCM on the thermal management process of cylindrical lithium-ion batteries. To increase the heat transfer rate and enhance the thermal management process, the authors used different types of nanoparticles: copper, silver, and iron oxide of sizes 70, 80–100, and 20–30 nm, respectively. Different samples were prepared for the cooling process by mixing each of the nanoparticles with paraffin PCM of melting temperature and latent heat of 39–45 °C and 239 KJ/Kg, respectively. Results showed that using silver nanoparticles achieves better thermal management than the other nanoparticles. Moreover, the study extends to investigate the effect of a metal matrix with PCM on the battery's temperature. Results showed that metal matrix PCM can decrease the maximum temperature difference by 70 % [208]. Kadam et al. used graphene nanoparticles to improve the thermal conductivity of Paraffin wax to thermally manage 12 cylindrical 18,650 li-ion cells. The PCM used in this experiment is RT-58 with a latent heat of 179 KJ/Kg and thermal conductivity of 0.2 W/m.k [228]. To increase the PCM thermal conductivity and produce a long, stable material for BTMS. Ling et al. used encapsulated inorganic PCM for battery thermal management. The authors used sodium acetate trihydrate-urea as a PCM and expended graphite as a microscale encapsulation material. Expanded graphite increases thermal conductivity and eliminates leakage problems. The authors further used organosilicon sealant to increase the stability of the material. The authors compared the prepared inorganic material with an organic material based on performance, price, and safety. Results showed that using inorganic enhanced material can minimize the maximum temperature of the module by 4 °C. In summary, the inorganic PCM composite has great thermo-physical properties, high stability, lower price, and is safe to use, which shows great potential in commercial use [229]. To enhance the thermal conductivity and improve the thermo-physical properties of PCM for BTMS applications, Zhang et al. added different amounts of aluminum nitride ranging from 0 wt% to 25 wt% with a fixed amount of expended graphite and epoxy resin. The PCM employed in this experiment had a melting point and latent heat of 50.6 °C and 239.35 kJ/kg, respectively. Results showed that the thermal conductivity increased from 1.48 W/m.k to 4.331 W/m.k when adding 20 wt% of aluminum nitride. Moreover, it was found that using the proposed CPCM, the maximum temperature of the cell decreased by 19.4 % at a 3C discharge rate [230].

In a tend to design an optimized BTMS. Youssef et al. used a NEPCM of 60 % paraffin and 40 % graphite with a melting temperature range and latent heat of 27–28 °C and 150 Kj/Kg, respectively. In this investigation, the authors investigate the thermal performance of a Li-ion battery with three different cooling strategies under varying loads, including fast discharge, periodic load, and real drive cycle. Results revealed that the maximum temperature of the battery under periodic load protocol was recorded to be 39.22 °C and 38.22 °C without cooling and with PCM cooling, respectively, and recorded to be 47.27 °C and 41.06 °C when applying fast discharge protocol, respectively. The authors investigated further by adding low-weight, high-thermal conductivity jute fibers to the composite PCM. It was seen that adding jute fibers can further decrease the temperature of the battery [231]. Jilte et al. numerically investigated the effect of using NEPCM with different configurations and combinations of PCMs on the battery's temperature. The authors used four types of PCM, including disodium sulfate decahydrate, eicosane, pureTemp37, and pureTemp44, all enhanced with aluminum oxide nanoparticles. The integration of the PCM with the battery was investigated in three different configurations. The first configuration inserts two different types of NEPCM in the radial direction, while the other two inserts the PCM in an axial direction. Results showed that with an optimized configuration, the battery temperature is maintained below 46 °C at an ambient temperature of 40 °C [232]. Luo et al. analyzed the effect of enhanced PCM using expanded graphite with 20 wt% on the thermal performance of lithium batteries under rapid heating and cooling functions ranging from −40 °C to 50 °C. Expanded graphite solves the PCM leakage issue and enhances thermal conductivity by 960 %. Results showed that the proposed method can maintain the temperature of the battery pack made up of 7 modules below 50 °C where it can reach 77 °C without PCM [233]. Li et al. conducted an optimization study of CPCM in BTMS. The main objective of this study is to optimize the mass of the PCM, satisfying two constraints, which are the maximum temperature difference in the BTMS and the working time of the BTMS under safe desired conditions. The authors used expended graphite to increase the thermal conductivity and solve the PCM leakage issue. The melting point and the latent heat are 44 °C and 879 Kj/Kg, respectively. A numerical study investigates different parameters and configurations, including cylindrical-shaped CPCMs with 1,2,3, and 4 batteries in 4 different configurations. Results showed that the proposed optimization method is solid and can be used to optimize the mass of the PCM of any similar systems [234]. Ghadbeigi et al. investigated the effect of pure paraffin and graphite-enhanced paraffin on the thermal performance of lithium batteries under cold temperatures. The temperature drop inside the module is measured for three configurations: a battery with no PCM, a battery with PCM, and a battery with NEPCM under 4 cold soak conditions. Results showed that the warming rate of PCM is slower by 29 % compared to NEPCM, which causes significant energy loss [235]. Greco et al. numerically investigated the effect of PCM integrated with compressed, expanded natural graphite on a cylindrical lithium battery's heat transfer and cooling process. A 1D numerical model is proposed and compared with a 3D numerical model to describe the transient thermal behavior of the battery and CPCM. After comparison, it was noted that using the 1D numerical method is easier and can yield the same results as the 3D model. Two types of PCM with the expended natural graphite were investigated under forced and natural convection. Results showed that the battery's temperature was kept under 40 °C for 2.9 h and can reduce the maximum temperature by 29.1 °C while applying forced convection [236].

Researchers use flexible PCM by adding polymers to enhance the heat transfer between the PCM and the battery. Flexible CPCM reduces thermal contact resistance. The battery can be tightly attached to the flexible CPCM, facilitated by thermally induced flexibility and shape recovery. This assembly increases the heat transfer and eliminates the requirement for thermal grease. Wu et al. developed an innovative PCM flexible composite with shape recovery that can be a good fit without needing thermal grease. The CPMC was prepared from pure paraffin RT44 with Olefin block copolymer and expanded graphite. Results showed that the temperature can be reduced by 28.8 °C compared to a non-cooled battery during a 2.5C discharge rate [237]. Wu et al. developed a flexible phase change material for battery thermal applications at room temperature in another study. In their approach, the authors combined copolyester thermoplastic elastomer with a polyether soft segment and integrated it with a PCM having a melting temperature range of 40–50 °C. The results demonstrated that the prepared CPCM exhibits flexibility across a wide temperature range, making it well suited for BTMS as it can reduce the maximum temperature by 5.4 °C compared to rigid CPCM [238]. Huang et al. prepared a flexible CPCM with anti-leakage and anti-vibration properties for BTMS. The authors mixed Styrene-Butadiene-Styrene with paraffin while heating at 150 °C and added expanded graphite. Results showed that the prepared material can maintain the battery's temperature below 50 °C at a 1 c discharge rate [239].

The added polymers provide thermal-induced flexibility and act as a supporting material that prevents leakage of liquid PCM. Moreover, the expended graphite increases the thermal conductivity and improves shape stability. Wu et al. composed a shape memory PCM using styrene-b-(ethylene-co-butylene)-b-styrene, triblock copolymer, paraffin, expanded graphite, and Fe3O4-modified graphene oxide to increase the thermal conductivity, restrict leakage issue, and make it compatible and have shape memory to enhance the heat transfer. Results showed that the thermal conductivity is effect by the size of expanded graphite. Reducing the size of the expanded graphite can decrease the thermal conductivity. Fe3O4-modified graphene oxide is shown to lower the thermal resistance between the paraffin and expanded graphite. Further results showed that the proposed material can keep the temperature difference within 3 °C at 3C discharge rate [240].

Lin et al. prepared an enhanced thermal conductivity, flexible, and more stable CPCM by mixing styrene-ethylene-propylene-styrene polymer with expended graphite at different mass ratios. The thermal conductivity of the prepared material achieved 10.019 W/m.k with a latent heat of 211.9 Kj/kg at an expended graphite loading of 30 wt%. Results showed that the prepared CPCM sustained the battery's temperature below 50 °C [241]. A similar study was conducted experimentally by Lin et al.; the battery temperature was maintained around 43 °C [242]. Zhang et al. [243] investigated the effect of mechanical vibration on BTMS integrated with NEPCM. The authors conducted experiments on pure paraffin and different NEPCM using graphite and graphene with different mass fractions. The experiments were conducted with 2–4 mm vibration amplitudes ranging from 10 to 30 Hz. Results showed that small vibrational amplitudes can enhance the heat transfer and accelerate the dispersion and collision of nanoparticles. It was also found that a small vibrational amplitude enhances the latent heat utilization time and increases the suitable temperature range of BTMS working time. However, it is noted that lower amplitudes to higher amplitudes are not suitable for enhancing heat transfer, where the optimal frequency was found to be 20 Hz. Table 11 summarizes some of the work mentioned in this section with its significant findings and highlights critical comments, gaps, and statements.

The literature concludes that nanoparticles are used in many experimental and numerical works in BTMS to increase thermal conductivity and solve the leakage issue of liquid PCM. It is noticed that the most used nanoparticle is expended graphite due to its high thermal conductivity, lightweight, and ability to make the PCM stable and solve leakage issues. Further, it is noted that researchers are using polymers to add flexibility to the PCM and act as supporting material. The flexibility of the PCM can reduce the thermal resistance, increasing the heat transfer between the battery and the PCM. There is limited work on other types of nanoparticles, so it is recommended that the effect of using metallic and metallic oxide nanoparticles in the application of BTMS be investigated further. It also recommends investigating the hybrid combination of different categories of nanoparticles and its applicability in BTMS.

Despite the clear thermal performance improvements offered by nano-enhanced PCMs, several operational challenges remain. One of the most critical is nanoparticle sedimentation during repeated melting and solidification cycles [244]. This phenomenon can lead to uneven distribution of nanoparticles, resulting in localized thermal degradation and loss of enhanced conductivity over time. Studies have reported that after multiple thermal cycles, the initial improvements in heat transfer rates can diminish, undermining the reliability of the thermal management system [245]. To address this, researchers have experimented with using surfactants, polymer-stabilized nanoparticles, and embedding nanoparticles in porous matrices or microcapsules. However, these solutions often add complexity or cost. Furthermore, long-term cycling studies are limited, and there is a need for systematic durability assessments under realistic battery operating conditions [246].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Advanced techniques, including fins and conductive foams for BTMS
ce:section.ce:para: Metallic foam is very lightweight and has a high thermal conductivity material that can enhance the thermal properties of the PCM. Its structure and porous shape allow the PCM to melt inside and become more stable, solving the leakage issue during melting. Different types of metallic foam include copper, aluminum, and nickel. At some point, using metallic foam in BTMS is better than integrating nanoparticles with PCM. However, using a hybrid combination can result in extraordinary outcomes.

Heyhat et al. numerically investigated the passive thermal management of 18,650 lithium-ion batteries using PCM. Nanoparticles, fins, and porous metal foam are used with the PCM to enhance the thermal performance. The authors considered n-eicosane with a melting point of 36 °C as a PCM, copper foam, and copper nanoparticles as metallic foam and nanoparticles. Different numerical models were considered in this study: local thermal non-equilibrium model and non-Darcy law used for simulating the melting process of NEPCM inside the porous material. Different parameters are investigated, and the effect of adding nanoparticles, the number of fins, and the effect of metal foam are analyzed. Results showed that PCM with metallic foam performs the best compared to NEPCM and PCM with fins. The metal foam with PCM can reduce the battery mean temperature by 6 k [247]. Alipanah et al. numerically analyzed three types of CPCM for BTMS. The first composite is pure octadecane. The second composite is gallium, and the third is octadecane, made of aluminum foam. The authors used a semi-implicit method for pressure-linked equation, temperature transforming model, and straggled grids to complete this study. Different parameters, such as aluminum foam porosity, heat fluxes, and thickness, are investigated. Results showed that metal foam can increase the discharge time by 7.3 times more than pure octadecane. Moreover, adding metal foam can increase the temperature uniformity of the battery. The maximum temperature difference at the battery surface decreases from 24 °C to 2 × 10–4 °C when using pure octadecane and octadecane with metal foam [248]. El Idi et al. conducted a numerical and experimental study to explore the effect of passive cooling using PCM in BTMS. The study aims to investigate the ability of pure PCM and metal foam PCM to handle the heat generated by lithium-ion batteries. Results showed that adding aluminum metal foam with PCM shows a higher ability than pure PCM to enhance heat transfer. Moreover, results showed that the thickness of the PCM is critical, and increasing the thickness has less of an effect on the battery surface temperature [155]. Sudhakaran et al. numerically explored parameters such as PCM type, thickness, heat transfer coefficient, and metallic foam percentage additives on the heat transfer inside BTMS. Four different PCMS were explored: capric acid, RT35, RT42, and RT5. The inclusion of PCM was at varying thicknesses of 2 mm, 4 mm, and 8 mm. Thea heat transfer coefficient varied between 5,7,9,11 W/m2k. in this study, the thermal conductivity of the PCM was enhanced by copper foam that was added at different percentages of 1,3,5 and 7 % by volume. Results showed that the heat transfer is influenced by 35.4 %, 35.13 %, 13.98, and 5.09 % for the type of PCM, thickness, heat transfer coefficient, and metallic foam volume, respectively [249]. Wang et al. compared the performance of different BTMSs with each other. The proposed BTMS is cooled using natural convection, paraffin, paraffin enhanced with graphite nanoparticles, and paraffin enhanced with graphite nanoparticles and nickel foam. Results showed that paraffin enhanced with graphite nanoparticles and paraffin enhanced by graphite nanoparticles and nickel foam can control the surface battery temperature. It can maintain the surface temperature above 0 °C for 40 min in extreme cold conditions at −20 °C [250]. Khaboshan et al. improved the cooling process of BTMS using three passive cooling methods: PCM, fins, and metal foam. The author investigated four different configurations of BTMS and investigated the effect of different fin shapes: trapezoidal, wavy, rectangular, I-shaped, and triangular fins. Results showed that the optimal configuration combining the 3 passive cooling methods could reduce the battery surface temperature by 3 k [251]. Huang et al. numerically and experimentally investigated the thermal performance of 25 parallel 18,650 lithium-ion battery modules cooled by PCM enhanced with metal foam and expanded graphite. Results showed that the optimal porosity of the metal foam is 94 %, achieving the module's lowest maximum temperature [252]. Sun et al. studied the cooling performance of PCM integrated with copper foam. The suggested combination of copper foam and PCM successfully increases heat dissipation and enhances the temperature uniformity within the battery module [253]. Similar results were seen in Reo et al. experimental investigation [146]. Table 12 summarizes different works on using PCM with metallic foam in BTMS.

Fins have a high capability of increasing the thermal conductivity of the PCM and enhancing the heat transfer in BTMS. Fins are important and are being used in many applications for thermal management. The shape of the fin, number, thickness, and material are very important to reach the optimum heat transfer. Different studies investigated the effects of fins on BTMS. Chen et al. numerically investigated, using COMSOL software, the thermal performance of a cylindrical battery submersed into a PCM chamber with several fins attached inside. The authors aimed to find the optimal BTMS by varying the system size and fin number. The PCM used inside the analysis is paraffin with a latent heat of 234.5 Kj/kg. The thickness of the PCM is 3 mm. Results showed that the optimal number of fins is 15 [254].

Mei et al. conducted an optimization study to find the best BTMS. The authors used PCM with expended graphite and fins. Results showed that paraffin PCM mixed with expended graphite preforms better than PCM integrated with fins. However, combining PCM with expended graphite and fins maximizes thermal management by reducing the battery temperature by 35.5 % [255]. To increase the thermal conductivity for PCM in BTMS. Sun et al. studied the effect of adding longitudinal fins with cylindrical rings on heat transfer. Results showed that using fins with the recommended configuration performs better in enhancing heat transfer than pure PCM [256]. Choudhari et al. investigated the effect of different fin shapes on the heat transfer inside BTMS. The authors studied rectangular fins, triangular fins, trapezoidal fins, I-shape fins, and T-shape fins. Moreover, the authors applied an optimization method in this study to find the best configuration for the highest heat transfer. Results showed that module temperature could be lowered by 2.38 % and 9.28 % at 2C and 3C discharge rates, respectively [257]. Li et al. explored petal-type BTMS with dual types of PCM. The author used two types of PCM with melting points and latent heat of 44 °C, 35.2 °C, and 255, 168 Kj/kg, respectively. The two PCMs are integrated around the cylindrical lithium-ion battery and separated using fins. Results showed that the optimized fin configuration can decrease the maximum temperature difference by 29.19 % at an ambient temperature of 40 °C [258]. Ping et al. studied the fin structure to enhance the thermal conductivity of the PCM integrated inside BTMS in a high-temperature environment. Different parameters were investigated, including the type of PCM, PCM thickness, fin thickness, and fin spacing in the cooling process. Results showed that the optimized configuration and structure can sustain the battery's temperature under 51 °C and an ambient temperature of 40 °C [259]. Ambekar et al. conducted an implicit finite volume numerical method to solve the governing equation concerning PCM integrated with fins inside BTMS. The thermal performance of 4S4P of 16 cell battery module is studied using a 2D numerical model. Five different fin structures are investigated at angle 45 o. Results showed that the proposed fins structure successfully sustained the temperature under 60 °C even after 2.5 discharges and charge cycles of 2C and 3C discharge rates [260]. Table 13 shows different configurations and fin shapes in BTMS.

Lv et al. enhanced the thermal conductivity of PCM by using low-density polyethylene and expended graphite integrated with fins in BTMS. Results showed that the proposed configuration can maintain the battery temperature below 50 °C and the temperature difference by a 5 °Cat discharge rate of 3.5C [266]. Weng et al. conducted an optimization study to find the optimal structure of internal fins combined with PCM for BTMS. The authors conducted a series of experimental investigations for different configurations with and without fins. Results showed that longitudinal fins are good in discharging heat from the bottom, while circular fins showed better heat conduction. Further results based on optimization showed that the maximum temperature of the battery is 29.1 °C at 1C discharge rate, which is 5.5 % less than that rectangular finned configuration [267]. Liu et al. analyzed the performance of a biomimetic honeycomb fin integrated with PCM inside BTMS. The findings indicate that incorporating a honeycomb fin effectively maintains the module temperature below 50 °C during the 10C discharging rate and leads to a 61 % rise in the temperature drop related to the configuration without a fin [268]. Another study was done by Liu et al. to investigate the effect of bionic leaf vain structure integrated with PCM in BTMS [261].

The characteristics of the porous carriers govern the effectiveness of CPCMs based on porous materials. Thus, the choice of porous carriers is important. It is essential to analyze the properties of porous carrier's factors such as porosity, specific surface area, specific pore volume, average pore size, compatibility with PCM, density, thermal conductivity, and toxicity and flammability. Currently, porous metal materials are extensively used in the application of lithium-ion BTMS [269].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hybrid integration of PCM with other cooling techniques for BTMS
ce:section.ce:para: Utilizing PCM in BTMS has demonstrated significant success, making it the optimal choice for achieving uniform cell temperatures in Battery.
