Hybrid BTMS combines passive cooling, employing PCM, with active cooling through forced cooling. Recently, hybrid cooling approaches for BTMS have become a focal point in research. Hybrid techniques, particularly those incorporating PCMs, are prioritized due to their impressive latent heat capacity, compact design, and cost-effectiveness. These approaches address the limitations of pure PCM, such as low thermal conductivity and heat discharge during solidification, and aim to enhance overall heat transfer efficiency. However, it is essential to note that these systems face challenges such as additional power consumption [270].

Different types of cooling techniques are used in BTMS, such as natural and forced convection, liquid cooling, heat pipes, thermoelectric coolers, and PCM. Table 14 shows the advantages and drawbacks of different hybrid techniques with PCM.

Liquid cooling is the most familiar technique due to its heat transfer capability and high thermal performance. Ping et al. numerically investigated the effect of integrating PCM and liquid cooling pipes on a prismatic Li-ion battery module temperature. The authors presented a non-uniform heat generation model to simulate the battery pack's surface temperature and investigate the effect of the coolant velocity, pipe position, and ambient temperature on the thermal management process. In this model, the authors used a 10 Ah battery and paraffin PCM of melting temperature and latent heat of 41–45 °C and 107.8 kJ/kg, respectively. Results showed that 4.5 °C can reduce the battery pack's temperature to 45 °C ambient temperature [271]. Bai et al. numerically investigated the impact of integrating PCM with a water-cooling plate on the heat transfer of the li-ion module. A 2D electro-thermal model was used to generate the non-uniform heat from the battery. Different parameters such as the height of the cooling plate, space between batteries, mass flow rate, flow direction, and PCM thermal conductivity and melting point were investigated. The study came to different conclusions; generally, the PCM integrated with a water plate can limit the maximum temperature and improve the uniformity of temperature distribution during five cycles [272]. Kong et al. investigated a novel BTMS integrated with PCM and liquid cooling under different ambient conditions. The authors numerically investigated the battery temperature under a 3C discharging rate followed by a 0.5C charging rate while applying the cooling system. Different parameters were studied during this study, including cell-to-cell spacing, cell-to-tube spacing, number of channels, and coolant velocity. The CPCM considered for this study consisted of pure PCM, lipid, and expanded graphite. Six commercial 21,700 Li-ion batteries were used in this study. The study revealed that the maximum surface temperature of the battery reached 41.1 °C at an ambient temperature of 30 °C and reduced the maximum temperature by 4 °C compared to non-cooled battery [185]. Yang et al. used a combination of lightweight cooling systems consisting of a liquid cooling system integrated with z-type cold plates and aluminum foam PCM. In this study, nine configurations were tested to find the most optimized design for BTMS. The findings reveal that the optimal design of the hybrid cold plate, weighing only half as much as the baseline, can achieve a greater than 50 % decrease in overall pumping power while maintaining equivalent cooling performance [273]. Yang et al. proposed a novel honeycomb BTMS shape with hexagonal cooling plate, bionic liquid mini channel and phase change material to improve the Li-ion battery performance. The proposed system is numerically investigated using CFD and a second-order equivalent circuit model. PEG1000, with a melting point of 34 °C, is used with a cylindrical 18,650 li-ion battery of 2.6 Ah. Results showed that the hexagonal cooling plate can further reduce the maximum temperature by 0.36 k compared to the rectangular cooling plate. Moreover, the authors proposed a neural network to estimate the inlet flow rate of the battery at different conditions [274]. Fan et al. investigated the combination of PCM with liquid cooling tesla valve on the temperature and performance of lithium battery pack. The authors proposed a thermal model verified by experimental data to analyze the battery module at a 2C discharge rate. Different parameters were studied, including battery spacing, number of channels, coolant velocity, pressure drop, and performance of the hybrid cooling system. Numerical results showed that using water and ethanol as coolant with an inlet velocity of 0.193 m/s affects the maximum temperature to become 33.12 °C with a pressure drop of 637.8 Pa. Further optimization of the proposed BTMS can reduce the total energy consumption by 79.9 % compared to conventional BTMS [275]. Cao et al. numerically studied the cooling of three Lithium-ion batteries using water and nano-encapsulated PCM inside a duct. Different parameters were investigated, such as Reynolds number, melting fraction, surface temperature, and heat transfer rate. Simulation results showed that increasing volume fraction and Reynolds number can increase the heat transfer rate by 13.6 % and 16.8 %, respectively. The battery temperature was between 35 °C and 45 °C [119]. Al-Rashed et al. used NEPCM and nanofluid to cool a lithium battery module. Rashed used water, copper oxide, and iron oxide as cooling liquids and nanoparticles, respectively, and used calcium chloride hexahydrate as a PCM. The thermophysical properties of the NEPCM and nanofluid are taken from the literature. Different conclusions came out, but in general, using NEPCM and nanofluid can increase the heat transfer rate and decrease melting and solidification time [276]. Hekmat et al. used a hybrid combination of PCM and liquid cooling channels to achieve highly uniform temperature distribution in lithium battery modules. The melting point and latent heat of the PCM used in this study are 28.15 °C and 179 Kj/Kg. The battery was a prismatic lithium battery with a 5.5 Ah capacity. Results showed that hybrid cooling can reduce the maximum temperature difference among the cell from 5.1 °C to 2.8 °C when the coolant inlet velocity is 0.0125 m/s [277]. Rostami et al. numerically investigated the effect of using a heat sink with a helical microchannel supplied with nanofluid and integrated with NEPCM. The chosen PCM for this study was calcium chloride hexahydrate, possessing a melting point and latent heat of 29 °C and 192 kJ/kg, respectively. To enhance the PCM, graphene nanoparticles were introduced. The nanofluid consisted of copper oxide nanoparticles mixed with water. The setup simulation was performed using COMSOL software across a range of Reynolds numbers from 100 to 500, and various parameters were systematically monitored and analyzed. Results indicated a dependence of the heat transfer coefficient between the PCM and nanofluid on the Reynolds number, showcasing an increase over time [278].

Thermoelectric cooling has been used recently in BTMS due to its cheap cost, compact size, large heat transfer ability, and high thermal efficiency. To investigate the battery performance and temperature in extreme environments under hybrid active and passive cooling systems. Liao et al. numerically proposed a 21,700 Cylindrical lithium-ion battery pack cooled with nano-enhanced phase change material and thermos electric modules. For this numerical investigation, the authors studied the effect of four different composites of paraffin wax. The first composite is pure paraffin PCM of melting temperature and latent heat of 44 °C and 226,100 J/Kg, respectively, and the other three composites were enhanced with expanded graphite with nanoparticle weight ratios of 3 wt%, 10 wt%, and 30 wt%, respectively. Results showed that battery temperature can be maintained lower than 318.15 k at a 3C discharging rate during extreme hot conditions. While in extreme cold conditions, the system can preheat the battery with a maximum rate of temperature rise of 1.333 k. overall, the proposed BTMS can provide an appropriate battery working condition of temperature ranging between 293.15 and 318.15 K under ambient temperatures of 268.15–313.15 K [279]. Liu et al. investigated the combination of a hybrid cooling approach on the thermal performance of lithium battery modules. The authors integrated fins, a thermoelectric cooler, and a liquid cooling plate with PCM. Different parameters were investigated numerically, such as fin thickness, which ranges from 2 mm to 8 mm, and thermoelectric cooler input current, which ranges from 1 to 6 A during the thermal process. Studies showed that the best thickness of the fin and the optimal input current are 4 mm and 3 A, respectively [280]. Heat pipes perform high thermal conductivity and have high heat transfer capability. Researchers are integrating heat pipes with PCM in BTMS to achieve high thermal efficiency. Wu et al. experimentally investigated the influence of PCM integrated with heat pipes on heat transfer and the battery pack's temperature. Forced convection produced by a fan further enhanced the proposed BTMS. Results showed that using PCM with heat pipes is feasible, which can lower the module's temperature below 50 °C and enhance the uniformity of temperature distribution [281]. Chen et al. numerically investigated the performance of BTMS based on PCM coupled with heat pipes. The authors investigated different parameters and applied optimization techniques to find the best PCM thickness. Results showed that 30 % of the temperature difference can be reduced after applying the optimization technique [282]. Peng et al. numerically investigated the thermal behavior of a battery system integrated with PCM and heat pipe. A 3D numerical model was prepared and explored against experimental data for a cylindrical li-ion module. Different parameters were investigated, including discharge rates and different PCM properties with expanded graphite. Results showed that different melting points of PCM do not highly affect the temperature difference between batteries, and using 12 wt% of expended graphite shows the best results in heat dissipation. However, the liquid fraction distribution inside the system is not uniform [283]. Leng et al. numerically explored the effect of combining heat pipe with PCM in BTMS. Results showed that improving the heat transfer coefficient and PCM melting temperature and reducing ambient temperature, heat input, and thermal conductivity can restrain internal energy distribution. Moreover, it was seen that PCM thickness has a small effect [284].

Applying fans or compressors to produce a forced convection effect is conducted by researchers due to the simplicity of the system and the availability of air. Safdari et al. numerically investigated the effect of PCM macro encapsulation in BTMS. In this study, the authors explore the effect of the passive cooling method using PCM and hybrid cooling using forced convection air and PCM. Three configurations for the PCM around the batteries were tested, consisting of circular, rectangular, and hexagonal shapes. Results showed that the circular configuration performs the best among all configurations [285]. Qi et al. numerically studied the PCM enclosure shape on the thermal performance of BTMS consisting of 9 cylindrical battery cells inside a duct exposed to forced air convection. Different enclosure shapes are investigated, including circular, horizontal, and vertical elliptical shapes. The authors reached different conclusions and noted that the outlet air temperature is minimum for the horizontal elliptical and maximum for the circular shape [286].

Different optimization techniques and energy-saving methods were used in BTMS. Weng et al. proposed a hybrid cooling system for energy saving in BTMS [287]. Leng et al. integrated heat pipe with PCM and applied optimization and energy-saving assessment for BTMS [288]. Al Miaari et al. reviewed different BTMSs that use machine learning. Different battery management systems mentioned in the review utilized PCM with machine learning. Combining machine learning with the hybrid system can achieve better results and higher efficiencies [289]. Table 15 summarizes different hybrid methods with PCM in cooling and thermal managing battery temperature.

Hybrid PCM-based systems integrating active cooling strategies exhibit notably different performance–complexity trade-offs. For example, PCM paired with liquid cooling achieved a ∼ 20 °C peak temperature reduction under 3C load, but at the expense of higher cost and system complexity due to pumps and coolant loops. In contrast, PCM–fin hybrids showed a 55 % temperature drop and maintained ΔT below 5 °C, offering superior thermal uniformity with moderate complexity [290]. Meanwhile, PCM coupled with heat pipes yielded 25–35 % peak reductions at 2–4C discharge, with balanced complexity and cost, outperforming PCM-only systems [291]. Quantitatively, performance ranges from ∼10–20 °C reduction (PCM–air) to >20 °C (PCM–liquid, PCM–heat pipe), and temperature non-uniformity can drop below ∼5 °C in well-designed hybrids. Qualitatively, liquid-cooled hybrids excel in thermal performance but incur higher system cost, weight, and maintenance; PCM–fin or PCM–heat-pipe systems offer strong uniformity with simpler architecture, making them ideal for applications prioritizing reliability over peak cooling.

From the literature, it can be concluded that hybrid cooling can achieve better thermal efficiency and battery thermal performance. However, when considering a hybrid combination, the system becomes more complicated and requires more parameters. Almost all reviewed papers were numerical investigations with less experimental work. It is essential to investigate different effective parameters numerically on thermal management; however, a real-life application should be experimentally investigated to draw a complete conclusion.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Advantages and challenges of PCM application in Li-ion batteries
ce:section.ce:para: Phase change materials (PCMs) would have immense advantages if incorporated in Li-ion batteries but are very challenging. On the positive side, PCMs are better at thermal management (TM), being capable of controlling battery temperature fluctuations during operation. This allows for enhanced battery performance and lifespan along with contributing to safety by reducing thermal runaway potential. PCMs also have the potential to optimize battery efficiency by maintaining the ideal temperature range. They can also enhance energy density, making it possible to create high-performance battery systems. There are, however, some challenges. Finding appropriate PCMs that can fulfill Li-ion battery specifications can be difficult and time-consuming. Incorporating PCMs into battery systems requires meticulous design to achieve good distribution without sacrificing structural integrity. Cost is also a factor since PCMs add to the manufacturing cost. Furthermore, PCMs constrain volume and weight, particularly with compact battery sizes. Last but not least, there are performance trade-offs to be tackled since PCMs will influence battery performance. Despite these limitations, research aims for the maximization of PCM integration into Li-ion batteries, satisfying performance, cost, and safety constraints to enable them to play their greatest potential role in improving battery performance and safety.

To provide a holistic understanding of phase change material (PCM)-based battery thermal management systems (BTMS), Table 16 summarizes key performance aspects of PCM-based BTMS, highlighting trade-offs across material type, thermal behavior, and integration challenges. While nano-enhanced and composite PCMs improve conductivity and thermal regulation, they also raise concerns about cost, stability, and complexity. The analysis shows that achieving reliable, safe, and cost-effective BTMS requires careful balancing of material performance, system design, and application-specific needs.

Moreover, Ag nanoparticle composites showed improved thermal performance [208]. Hybrid carbon-based PCMs for TM of Li-ion battery modules were investigated and showed that the use of hybrid thermal conductivity fillers can significantly enhance thermal conductivity and cooling effect, and some composite ratios achieve the optimum cooling effects [298]. Finally, a review of literature on battery TM systems using PCMs for electric vehicles (EVs) has reported that PCMs could be a cheaper and less maintenance-demanding option compared to traditional air or coolant circuits with the potential for passive or semi-passive systems enabling quasi-autonomous operations [171].

Especially in EV applications, incorporating PCMs in Li-ion battery TM techniques offers an attractive way of scaling up batteries' safety, reliability, and efficiency. From the investigation, it emerges that various PCMs and geometries can be tailored to particular TM requirements, where ongoing innovations in material and system design continuously advance this technology. Table 17 summarizes PCMs' influences on the thermophysical properties of Li-ion batteries.

Utilization of PCMs in the TM of Li-ion batteries offers a potential means to enhance their energy efficiency and safety. Adding materials such as metal foams, carbon fibers, and graphene can significantly increase the thermal conductivity and heat distribution of PCMs. Hybrid systems of PCMs and other conductive materials appear particularly effective. However, the performance of PCM-based systems is environmentally dependent, and there are certain low-temperature limitations that need to be surmounted. In general, the research suggests that the composition and structure of PCM-based BTMS must be optimized to maximize the advantages of Li-ion battery applications.

The strength, safety, and lifespan of lithium-ion (Li-ion) batteries are critical for their application in electric vehicles (EVs) and other high-demand systems. Phase change materials (PCMs) have been extensively investigated to enhance thermal management (TM), which directly impacts battery performance, longevity, and safety. Long-duration experiments indicate that PCM cooling can reduce thermal degradation and improve cycle life by suppressing cell inconsistency and slowing cathode deterioration [302]. Additionally, PCMs provide passive protection against thermal runaway by absorbing excess heat and lowering the peak temperatures experienced by adjacent cells, thereby preventing failure propagation [303]. In cold environments, PCMs can retain and release thermal energy to warm cells, improving discharge capacity and voltage, albeit sometimes slowing the initial temperature rise [304]. The incorporation of conductive additives and hybrid system designs further enhances PCM performance by improving thermal conductivity and enabling adaptability to varying ambient conditions and load profiles. These advancements not only improve thermal regulation but also significantly boost battery system reliability and durability.

However, despite their benefits, PCMs face several operational challenges. During repeated phase transitions, traditional PCMs may leak, deform, or collapse due to their inherent flowability and volume change during melting [305]. To address these limitations, researchers have developed composite PCMs by incorporating structural support materials with higher melting points or thermosetting properties. These materials stabilize the PCM and prevent leakage, often using porous substrates such as expanded vermiculite, polymers, perlite, diatomite, and various carbon-based materials. Particularly, carbon-based supports (e.g., carbon fiber, carbon foam, CNTs, graphene, and expanded graphite) enhance both mechanical integrity and thermal conductivity [19]. Additionally, polymer frameworks—especially thermoplastics like polyvinyl alcohol and polyethylene—can adsorb and encapsulate PCMs via micro- or nano-scale pore networks, further reducing leakage and improving strength.

Another widely adopted method is microencapsulation, where PCMs are enclosed within microcapsules with organic or inorganic shells. This approach not only reduces leakage but also increases the surface area for heat exchange and buffers volume changes during phase transitions [306]. Microencapsulated PCMs (MePCMs), ranging from nanometers to hundreds of microns in size, offer enhanced stability and adaptability in battery systems. Nevertheless, thermal safety remains a concern, particularly for organic PCMs, which are flammable and can damage modules upon melting. As a result, the development of PCM materials with high thermal stability and inherent flame retardancy has become a key research priority to ensure safe, efficient, and long-lasting thermal management solutions in modern Li-ion battery systems [158].

Phase change materials (PCMs) are widely explored for enhancing the thermal management (TM) of lithium-ion (Li-ion) batteries, particularly in electric vehicles and other high-demand energy storage systems. One of the key advantages of PCMs is their ability to offer passive thermal protection at low cost, low weight, and with minimal system complexity [303]. For instance, experimental studies have demonstrated that PCM-based composites (e.g., PCC™) can suppress thermal runaway and reduce the peak temperature of neighbouring cells by over 60 °C, confirming their value in safety enhancement. Additionally, the integration of hybrid thermal conductivity fillers has been shown to improve the thermal conductivity of PCMs by up to 264 %, reducing the battery module's peak temperature by as much as 16.33 °C while maintaining safe temperature uniformity (ΔT ≤ 5 °C) [304]. Hybrid cooling strategies that combine PCMs with air or dielectric fluids have also proven more effective than using either medium alone, offering further 6 °C temperature reduction in simulation studies, especially beneficial at high discharge rates [298].

Emerging flexible, form-stable composite PCMs (CPCMs) have demonstrated further benefits, including reduced thermal contact resistance and improved adaptability under dynamic loads. One study showed that under a 10C discharge rate, CPCMs reduced battery temperature by 18 °C, with effectiveness depending on the phase change temperature, ambient conditions, and discharge profile. However, while promising, PCM-based BTMs face several technical limitations that currently restrict their widespread use.

Key challenges include low intrinsic thermal conductivity, early melting tendencies, and leakage or shape instability during phase transitions. Furthermore, flammability remains a critical issue—especially with paraffin-based PCMs—which can worsen battery fires under localized heating by releasing additional heat once ignited. In such scenarios, PCMs may accelerate rather than suppress thermal runaway, directly undermining their intended safety function. There is a pressing need to develop PCM materials with greater thermal stability, non-flammability, and self-healing capabilities to ensure BTMs are not only effective but intrinsically safe. The lack of system-level hazard mitigation strategies and resilience mechanisms remains an under-researched area in PCM design [307].

Additionally, inconsistencies in reported optimal PCM thickness, often due to varying battery types, module designs, and PCM compositions, highlight the absence of standardized testing protocols—complicating the comparison of results across studies. Most currently used PCMs are derived from petrochemical sources and are often environmentally hazardous and corrosive. This has prompted interest in bio-based PCMs, particularly those derived from vegetable oils or fatty acids, which offer similar latent heat storage capacities while being more sustainable [294].

To fully harness the benefits of PCMs, future research must focus on the development of dual-functional and multifunctional BTMs that not only manage temperature but also offer system resilience, self-protection, and fire suppression. Coupling these materials with intelligent sensing and control, driven by advances in information technologies, could significantly enhance their performance and reliability. Despite current challenges, the financial and operational benefits of PCM-based BTMs are substantial, and their value is expected to grow with the increasing demand for robust and efficient thermal management solutions in the Li-ion battery industry [308].

It is important to note that several studies have reported contradictory results regarding the effectiveness of nano-enhanced PCMs. For instance, while Al₂O₃- and CuO-based composites have shown marked improvements in thermal conductivity [308], others using graphene or carbon nanotube additives observed diminished latent heat capacity and uneven melting behavior [294]. These inconsistencies primarily stem from differences in nanoparticle dispersion quality, PCM–filler interfacial bonding, and thermal cycling methodologies. Moreover, hybrid PCM systems integrating metal foams or liquid-assisted layers often exhibit improved short-term temperature regulation but compromised long-term reliability due to phase segregation or material degradation. A critical comparison of these findings indicates that the contradictory outcomes arise not from intrinsic material limitations but from the lack of standardized evaluation protocols. Establishing unified testing parameters and characterization benchmarks will thus be crucial for reconciling these disparities and guiding reproducible PCM-based BTMS design.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Battery heating and PCM behavior under low-temperature conditions
ce:section.ce:para: While most PCM studies focus on thermal protection under high-temperature conditions, cold-climate performance and battery preheating are also critical. In sub-zero environments (e.g., −10 °C to −30 °C), lithium-ion batteries suffer from increased internal resistance, reduced capacity, and safety risks such as lithium plating [309,310]. To address this, some researchers have developed hybrid preheating systems that combine PCMs with active heating elements. For example, a model combining PCM with heat films effectively warmed prismatic cells before discharge [311], while another integrated PCM-based intermittent heating systems maintained battery capacity at −30 °C, extending operational capability [312]. Additionally, a comprehensive review identifies advanced strategies that utilize AC heaters, PTC water heaters, or thermoelectric elements in conjunction with PCM insulation as effective preheating solutions [313]. These studies highlight that cold-start conditions require adaptive PCM strategies, combining passive thermal storage with controlled active heating, and that integrating these approaches into BTMS design is a promising yet underexplored area.

The continuous evolution of lithium-ion battery technologies, driven by the need for enhanced performance, safety, and longevity, demands parallel advancements in thermal management systems. Phase change materials (PCMs) have demonstrated significant potential in this context due to their passive cooling capability and high latent heat storage [294]. However, for PCM-based battery thermal management systems (BTMS) to achieve widespread commercial adoption, innovative enhancements in material performance, system integration, and intelligent optimization are critical [295]. This section focuses on three major emerging directions: (1) the use of machine learning and artificial intelligence for optimizing PCM-BTMS configurations, (2) the development of multifunctional PCMs with properties such as flame retardancy and structural reinforcement, and (3) cost-benefit analysis frameworks to evaluate the economic viability of these advanced thermal management solutions in real-world applications [314]. The research primarily focused on examining the effectiveness of graphene-coated nickel foam impregnated with paraffin as a TM (Fig. 37 ). The results demonstrated a significant 23-fold rise in thermal conductivity compared to pure paraffin. This novel material also affects the melting and freezing temperatures and the latent and specific heat of the PCM, resulting in a 17 % lower battery surface temperature rise compared to nickel foam alone [315,316].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Machine learning for optimizing PCM-based thermal management systems
ce:section.ce:para: Recent progress in machine learning (ML) and data-driven modeling has opened new possibilities for enhancing the design and operation of PCM-integrated BTMS. Traditional optimization techniques, typically based on iterative simulation using finite element or computational fluid dynamics (CFD) models, are computationally intensive, time-consuming, and often limited to a narrow design space. In contrast, ML-based approaches enable fast, adaptive, and multi-objective optimization using predictive modeling, surrogate algorithms, and real-time system feedback [293].

One of the most promising applications of ML in PCM-based BTMS is design parameter optimization. Researchers have used supervised learning techniques such as support vector regression (SVR), random forest regression, and artificial neural networks (ANNs) to model the nonlinear relationships between key design variables (e.g., PCM type, thickness, distribution, nanoparticle doping ratio) and performance outcomes like maximum cell temperature, thermal uniformity, and energy efficiency. For instance, some research trained an ANN model using simulation data from hundreds of BTMS configurations to predict thermal behavior with high accuracy [293]. The model was then coupled with a genetic algorithm to optimize PCM layout and geometry, reducing the maximum battery temperature by 15 % compared to conventional designs.

Another frontier is the application of reinforcement learning (RL) in thermal management control. Unlike fixed-parameter systems, RL agents can learn optimal control policies by interacting with the system environment and receiving feedback on temperature distributions, thermal gradients, and energy consumption. This is particularly valuable for hybrid systems combining PCMs with active components like fans or pumps, where dynamic control is essential [298]. By integrating RL with sensor networks and digital twins of battery modules, adaptive control strategies can be developed that respond in real-time to load cycles, ambient temperature fluctuations, and battery aging characteristics. Moreover, ML-based techniques are now being employed for fault detection and thermal anomaly prediction in PCM-integrated battery packs [317]. Predictive models trained on historical temperature and voltage profiles can detect early signs of localized overheating, PCM degradation, or phase separation. These tools enhance the reliability of BTMS by enabling proactive maintenance and failure prevention, which is crucial for high-risk applications such as electric vehicles and aerospace systems [318].

Despite the promising progress, several challenges must be addressed. First, the availability of large, high-quality datasets for training ML models is limited, especially for newer PCM formulations and configurations [319]. Collaborative open-source databases and standardized testing protocols can help mitigate this barrier. Second, model generalization remains a concern, ML models trained on one battery system may not accurately predict outcomes in different geometries, operating conditions, or PCM types [320]. Hybrid modeling approaches that combine physical laws with data-driven learning (physics-informed ML) offer a potential solution to this problem. Lastly, the integration of ML algorithms into battery management systems (BMS) requires robust hardware, firmware, and communication protocols to ensure safe, real-time deployment [316]. The incorporation of machine learning into PCM-BTMS design and operation is a transformative development, promising faster innovation cycles, smarter systems, and better thermal control outcomes with reduced energy overhead and design costs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Multifunctional PCMs and commercial viability considerations
ce:section.ce:para: While conventional PCMs offer high latent heat and isothermal heat absorption, their practical application in lithium-ion battery packs remains constrained by several limitations, including poor thermal conductivity, flammability, low structural integrity, and sensitivity to environmental degradation [140]. The development of multifunctional PCMs, designed to address these challenges while providing added safety, structural, or environmental benefits, represents a key frontier in thermal management research. One prominent direction is the integration of flame-retardant additives or structures into PCM formulations. Traditional paraffin-based PCMs are highly flammable, posing significant safety risks in case of leakage, overcharging, or short-circuit-induced thermal runaway. Recent research has explored the incorporation of materials such as expandable graphite, ammonium polyphosphate, organo‑phosphorus compounds, and intumescent nanostructures into PCM matrices to inhibit combustion and delay flame propagation [208]. For instance, researcher developed a paraffin-graphite composite PCM with a flame spread time exceeding 300 s, satisfying UL-94 V-0 standards for flame resistance. These composites not only improve thermal safety but also enhance conductivity, enabling more uniform temperature distribution across battery modules [321].

Another innovative class of multifunctional PCMs includes mechanically robust composites that provide structural support in addition to thermal regulation. By embedding metal foams, carbon nanotube networks, or graphene aerogels, these advanced PCMs exhibit improved mechanical strength, anti-leakage properties, and resistance to volume change during phase transitions [252]. Such properties are particularly desirable for space-constrained applications like electric vehicles, drones, and portable electronics, where mechanical integrity must be preserved under vibration, shock, and long-term cycling [322]. In parallel, self-healing and recyclable PCMs are being investigated to improve the sustainability and service life of BTMS. Encapsulation techniques using polyurethane or silicone shells allow for the reusability of PCM modules and facilitate end-of-life recycling. Self-healing polymer-based PCMs, activated by heat or pressure, can autonomously repair microcracks, thus extending the thermal and mechanical lifespan of the system. Moreover, bio-based and biodegradable PCM candidates (such as fatty acid esters and sugar alcohols) are gaining attention due to their lower environmental impact compared to petroleum-based counterparts [323].

While material innovations are essential, the commercial feasibility of PCM-BTMS depends on a thorough cost-benefit evaluation. This includes the analysis of material costs, fabrication complexity, performance improvement, and lifecycle advantages compared to traditional cooling methods [324]. Cost-benefit models must account for not only direct expenses (e.g., raw PCM, manufacturing, integration) but also indirect benefits such as improved battery lifespan, lower warranty claims, and enhanced customer safety perception. For instance, studies have shown that even a 10 °C reduction in peak battery temperature can extend cycle life by 40–60 %, reducing long-term costs in fleet and grid-scale applications [325]. Emerging frameworks for techno-economic analysis (TEA) provide systematic tools to quantify these trade-offs. For example, a comparative TEA between air cooling, liquid cooling, and PCM-enhanced hybrid systems showed that while initial investment in PCM-BTMS was 20–30 % higher, the payback period was reduced by up to 18 months due to extended battery lifespan and reduced thermal management energy consumption. Sensitivity analyses further revealed that nanomaterial cost and manufacturing scalability were the most critical factors affecting system viability [325]. Zhang et al. [145] studied a novel heat pipe assisted separation type battery thermal management system based on PCM. Their proposed system offers a more suitable temperature and can better reduce the temperature imbalance within the battery pack than other methods. Jilte et al. [232] studied a novel battery thermal management system using nano-enhanced phase change materials. They found that the PCMs layered in a radial and axial direction showed lower cell temperature in the former case. Luo et al. [206] experimentally investigated on a novel phase change material composites coupled with graphite film used for thermal management of lithium-ion batteries.

Finally, standardization and regulatory alignment are key enablers for the commercial deployment of PCM-based systems. Establishing unified metrics for PCM performance (such as effective thermal conductivity, latent heat utilization ratio, flammability index, and integration complexity) would allow for fair comparison across materials and vendors. Certification standards (e.g., UL, IEC, ISO) that recognize multifunctional properties (e.g., flame retardancy, recyclability) would also accelerate adoption in electric mobility and stationary storage markets.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Future research directions for PCM-based BTMS
ce:section.ce:para:
The specific future research directions and open areas in the field of PCM-based battery thermal management systems (BTMS), aligning with recent trends and emerging challenges:
1.
Advanced Material Development
•
Design of high-conductivity PCMs using hybrid fillers (e.g., graphene + metal oxides).
•
Development of bio-based and recyclable PCMs for sustainable and eco-friendly systems.
•
Exploration of non-flammable and self-healing PCMs to enhance safety and lifespan.
2.
Nano-Enhanced and Composite PCMs
•
Investigation into dispersion stability of nanoparticles within PCM matrices.
•
Optimization of nanoparticle type, shape, and concentration for thermal performance.
•
Study of long-term thermal cycling effects on composite PCM degradation.
3.
Hybrid Cooling System Integration
•
Design and modeling of PCM-active hybrid systems (PCM + liquid cooling or heat pipes).
•
Real-time control algorithms for hybrid systems to adapt to load fluctuations.
•
Development of compact and lightweight hybrid modules for EVs and drones.
4.
Machine Learning and Optimization
•
Use of machine learning to predict thermal behavior and optimize PCM layout/configuration.
•
Integration of physics-informed neural networks for modeling thermal interactions.
•
Implementation of AI-based control systems in real-time battery operation.
5.
Techno-Economic and Lifecycle Assessment
•
Cost-benefit analysis of nano-enhanced and hybrid PCM systems in commercial products.
•
Comprehensive lifecycle assessment (LCA) of PCM materials and BTMS architectures.
•
Development of standardized metrics for evaluating BTMS economic and environmental impact.
6.
Thermal Safety and Diagnostics
•
Real-time thermal diagnostics and anomaly detection using embedded sensors.
•
Studies on PCM performance under abuse conditions (overcharging, short circuits).
•
Assessment of thermal runaway suppression capabilities of novel PCM structures.
7.
System-Level Implementation and Scalability
•
Evaluation of modular and scalable PCM-BTMS designs for large battery packs.
•
Investigation into integration challenges within vehicle or grid-scale systems.
•
Testing under diverse climatic and load conditions to ensure universal applicability.

This review provides a comprehensive synthesis of recent advancements and emerging innovations in phase change material (PCM)-based battery thermal management systems (BTMS) for lithium-ion batteries. The novelty of this work lies in its integrated analysis of material development, hybrid system configurations, intelligent optimization techniques, and techno-economic perspectives, areas that are often addressed in isolation in prior literature. The primary aim of the review was to evaluate the current state-of-the-art and identify promising future directions that can enhance the safety, performance, and scalability of PCM-BTMS technologies. The main outcomes of the review:
•
The review classifies PCMs into pure, nano-enhanced, and hybrid systems, clarifying their thermal behavior, limitations, and integration strategies.
•
Nano-enhanced PCMs significantly improve thermal conductivity and temperature uniformity compared to pure PCMs but require careful control of dispersion and stability.
•
Hybrid BTMS that combine PCMs with active cooling components offer superior thermal performance, especially under high heat flux and dynamic loading conditions.
•
Machine learning and data-driven modeling approaches are emerging as powerful tools to optimize PCM-BTMS design, predict thermal behavior, and enable adaptive control.
•
Multifunctional PCMs with added features such as flame retardancy, structural reinforcement, and recyclability are promising for enhancing safety and environmental performance.
•
Cost-benefit and lifecycle assessments are increasingly essential for evaluating the commercial viability of PCM-based solutions, particularly for EVs and grid storage applications.

This work is important because it not only consolidates fragmented research findings across material science, thermal engineering, and energy systems, but also directs attention to underexplored opportunities such as intelligent control and standardization. By bridging current gaps in the literature and highlighting future research needs, this review supports the development of safer, smarter, and more sustainable thermal management technologies, which are crucial for the widespread deployment of lithium-ion batteries in emerging energy systems.
